original_prompt:
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.

phase_discussions.title: Prompt Contract
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice actually showed up this round (Codex's CLI wasn't available), but Claude's pass was thorough and self-consistent, and there's nothing here that's actually contested — it's a solo read of an unambiguous spec, not a live disagreement between parties. The scope is clean: this phase builds Somna only, not the 5-app portfolio ideation/selection that already happened upstream. Where the spec left things soft (MVP vs. "production-ready," which iOS-native features are hard-required vs. bonus), Claude named the assumption explicitly rather than picking silently, which is exactly what this phase is supposed to do. Nothing here needs a second round of arguing — it's ready to lock in.

## Final Output

**Original prompt:** preserved verbatim above (Somna app spec + parent portfolio prompt for context only — the portfolio-level ideation/selection/uniqueness-against-5-apps work is out of scope for this child build; it was already done one level up).

**Hard requirements:**
- Real, compiling Xcode project with full SwiftUI source in `app_build` — no spec doc, no partial scaffold.
- SwiftData persistence for nightly sleep logs and morning check-ins; fully local-first, no backend dependency.
- All core workflows present and functional: bedtime/wake logging, morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags, weekly recap card, plain-English correlation insights, optional read-only HealthKit import.
- Correlation engine: on-device rule-based/statistical only (no LLM, no hardcoded fake insights), with a real, sample-size-gated "still learning" state — not cosmetic, must actually suppress false-confidence output early on.
- HealthKit: read-only, optional, app must be fully functional with zero permission granted — no crashes, no blocking, no infinite re-prompt loops.
- StoreKit paywall (local `.storekit` config is acceptable for testing) gating: full tag-correlation engine, unlimited history, Live Activity. Free tier keeps logging, streaks, weekly recap.
- Live Activity (bedtime countdown) and WidgetKit widget (last night's score + tonight's target) — treated as required v1 scope per the spec's own "v1 Build Scope"/"iOS-Native Features" listing, but allowed to degrade gracefully (placeholder/last-known state) rather than block ship if they get disproportionately fiddly.
- ShareLink on the recap card.
- Design system must actually match "nocturnal calm": indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward dawn by time-of-day, soft-glow rounded cards, moon-phase icon motif — default SwiftUI styling is non-compliant.
- Real empty/loading/success/error states everywhere reachable, especially the insights screen's insufficient-data state.
- Accessibility: Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast.

**Non-goals:**
- No CloudKit/server sync in v1 — only a repository boundary that allows it later.
- No sleep-stage detection, audio recording, wearable/ring integration, ML sleep-staging.
- No LLM-generated insight text — template strings driven by real computed stats.
- No AR.
- No re-running the 5-app ideation/selection — already decided upstream.

**Production-readiness definition:** all v1-scope items work end-to-end on-device, no crashes, real persistence, a real (simple) correlation algorithm on real logged data, a functioning StoreKit paywall, polished UI matching the design direction. Live Activity/widget/HealthKit can be architecturally present but don't need to be flawless across every OS edge case — core logging/check-in/correlation/paywall loop is the non-negotiable bar.

**Decision rules for later phases:**
- If ambiguous, name the assumption in output rather than silently choosing — e.g., Live Activity/widget are hard-required, HealthKit is soft-required, exactly as reasoned above.
- Core workflows and paywall ship no matter what; Live Activity/widget degrade gracefully rather than jeopardizing the core loop.
- Explicitly test/reason about: zero-data first launch, wake-before-bedtime or incomplete log pairs, HealthKit permission denial, StoreKit product load failure (offline/sandbox), midnight-crossing/DST time math, and that the "still learning" threshold is a real gate, not just a label next to a bogus number.

>

Now, treating this as an execution contract, because that's what this phase exists to produce, and I want to flag where the spec is soft enough that a later phase could quietly drift.

The single app this build is actually responsible for is Somna. The parent portfolio prompt talks about 5 apps, ideation of 10+ candidates, judging viral potential, uniqueness against ~50 other apps — none of that applies at this phase. This child project already has its app selected. If a later phase starts re-litigating "should this be a different concept" or trying to brainstorm alternate ideas because the parent prompt says "generate 10 candidates," that's a scope violation — the candidate generation and selection already happened one level up. This phase's job is to build Somna as specified, not re-derive it.

Hard requirements I see, stated so a later phase can be checked against them:

- Real, compiling Xcode project with full SwiftUI source in `app_build` — a spec doc or partial scaffold is a failure per the parent build rules, and that rule binds this child too.
- SwiftData persistence for nightly sleep logs and morning check-ins. Local-first, no backend dependency for v1.
- Core workflows must all be present and functional: bedtime/wake logging, morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags, weekly recap card, correlation insights list, optional HealthKit import.
- Correlation engine must be on-device, rule-based/statistical — not a fake LLM call, not a hardcoded insight. And it must have an honest "still learning" / insufficient-data state gated on some minimum sample size, not just cosmetic — this is explicitly called out as a key risk in the spec, so I'd treat "ship false-confidence insights on day 2 of data" as a contract violation, not a nice-to-have.
- HealthKit import, if attempted, must be read-only, optional, and the app must be fully functional with zero HealthKit permission granted — this needs an actual test path (deny permission, confirm nothing crashes or shows blank states pretending to be errors).
- StoreKit paywall for Somna+ gating the correlation engine (full tag correlations), unlimited history, and Live Activity — free tier still gets logging, streaks, weekly recap.
- Live Activity for bedtime countdown and a WidgetKit widget showing last night's score + tonight's target — these are "iOS-native features," I read them as required v1 scope, not optional stretch, since they're listed under "v1 Build Scope" explicitly.
- ShareLink on the recap card.
- Design system must match the "nocturnal calm gradient" direction — indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward dawn tones by time-of-day context, soft-glow rounded cards, moon-phase icon motif. This is a testable visual requirement, not decoration — if the shipped UI is default SwiftUI System Blue lists, that's non-compliant with spec, not just "less polished."
- Empty/loading/success/error states per the global build rules, applied to every screen that can reach them — specifically the correlation insights screen has a real empty/insufficient-data state that's part of the product logic, not boilerplate.
- Accessibility: Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, 44pt tap targets, WCAG AA contrast — inherited from the parent portfolio's "SECOND PRIORITY" section, and I'd hold this app to it even though it's a solo child build, since it was in the design bar that produced this spec in the first place.

Non-goals, explicit, so nobody expands scope:
- No cloud sync, no server backend, no CloudKit implementation in v1 — only a repository boundary/interface that would let CloudKit be swapped in later. Building actual CloudKit sync now is over-scope.
- No sleep-stage detection, no audio recording, no ring/wearable integration, no ML sleep-staging model. The spec explicitly rejects this as "sensor-heavy."
- No LLM-generated insights — the correlation engine is specified as on-device statistics, not an on-device or remote LLM. If a later phase reaches for CoreML/LLM to generate the "plain-English" insight text, that would fight the spec's own stated rationale (battery-safe, privacy, honest). Template-based plain-English strings driven by computed statistics are the correct interpretation, not generative text.
- No AR. Spec says none required.
- Re-running ideation/selection of the 5-app portfolio — not this phase's job, already done upstream.

Production-readiness definition, since "MVP" and "production-ready" are in tension in this spec (parent prompt says "0 to production-ready," child says "MVP build"): I'd resolve this by naming the assumption rather than picking silently — production-ready here means the v1 build scope items all work end-to-end on-device with no crashes, real persistence, a real (if simple) correlation algorithm operating on real logged data, a functioning StoreKit paywall (even if using a local `.storekit` config for testing, not necessarily live App Store Connect products), and polished UI matching the design direction — but it does NOT mean every stretch feature (Live Activity, widget, HealthKit) needs to be flawless if they are architecturally optional per the spec's own "Key Risks" section. HealthKit specifically is explicitly bonus-only. Live Activity and widget are listed in v1 scope though, so I'd hold those to the same bar as core workflows, not treat them as bonus.

Biggest risk to my own read here: I'm inferring that Live Activity and Widget are hard-required because they're under "v1 Build Scope" and "iOS-Native Features," while HealthKit is soft-required because the spec calls it "optional" and "bonus only" in two places. Someone could argue Live Activity should also be soft-scoped since it depends on real bedtime data existing and Live Activities are notoriously fiddly to get right (ActivityKit entitlements, Dynamic Island rendering, background refresh reliability) — if a later build phase burns disproportionate time chasing a working Live Activity and it jeopardizes shipping the core logging/check-in/correlation loop, the decision rule should be: core workflows and paywall ship no matter what; Live Activity and widget degrade gracefully (e.g., widget shows placeholder/last-known-state, Live Activity feature can be present in code but not required to be bulletproof across every OS edge case) rather than blocking completion.

Failure modes I'd want later phases to explicitly test or at least reason about, since nobody else may be wearing this hat: what happens on first launch with zero data (does the recap card and insights screen show a real empty state, not a crash or an empty white screen); what happens when a user logs a wake time before a bedtime, or logs bedtime for one night but skips wake for three nights (data integrity in SwiftData model, does the correlation engine choke on nil/incomplete pairs); what happens when the user denies HealthKit permission — does anything block or crash, or silently retry the permission prompt in a loop; what happens if the paywall's StoreKit products fail to load (no network, sandbox misconfigured) — does the whole app become unusable or does free tier remain usable; timezone/DST edge cases in bedtime/wake time math since sleep sessions cross midnight; and whether "still learning" threshold is actually enforced with a real sample-size check rather than just a string that says "still learning" while showing a bogus percentage anyway.

The scope is clean: this phase builds Somna only, not the 5-app portfolio ideation/selection that already happened upstream. Where the spec left things soft (MVP vs. "production-ready," which iOS-native features are hard-required vs. bonus), Claude named the assumption explicitly rather than picking silently, which is exactly what this phase is supposed to do. Nothing here needs a second round of arguing — it's ready to lock in.

## Final Output

- ShareLink on the recap card.
- Design system must actually match "nocturnal calm": indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward dawn by time-of-day, soft-glow rounded cards, moon-phase icon motif — default SwiftUI styling is non-compliant.
- Real empty/loading/success/error states everywhere reachable, especially the insights screen's insufficient-data state.
- Accessibility: Dynamic Type, VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast.

**Non-goals:**
- No CloudKit/server sync in v1 — only a repository boundary that allows it later.
- No sleep-stage detection, audio recording, wearable/ring integration, ML sleep-staging.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. The output preserves the original prompt (with correct scoping: full spec reproduced, portfolio-level ideation/selection explicitly marked out-of-scope for this child build), and covers all five required sections concretely.

Strengths:
- Hard requirements are specific and checkable (e.g., "real, sample-size-gated 'still learning' state — not cosmetic," "app must be fully functional with zero HealthKit permission granted") rather than restating the spec.
- The MVP-vs-production-ready and Live-Activity/widget-required-vs-HealthKit-optional ambiguities are resolved by naming the assumption explicitly, exactly as the phase rules require, rather than silently picking an interpretation.

- Decision rules carry forward the concrete failure modes identified in discussion (zero-data launch, incomplete log pairs, HealthKit denial, StoreKit load failure, DST/midnight math) so a build-verification phase has an actual test list, not just vibes.

The "multi-app quantity" acceptance check is satisfied by correctly noting it's inapplicable at this scope (single selected child app) rather than fabricating portfolio-level detail — the right call, not an omission.

No repair needed; ready to lock in.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Product Research
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Codex didn't weigh in again this round, but the single Claude pass is thorough, internally consistent, and directly answers everything this phase is supposed to produce — there's no live disagreement to resolve, just one solid take that names its own assumptions. It correctly treats the prior phase's comparable-apps knowledge as informed judgment rather than claiming real market research was done, flags concrete risks beyond what was already in the prompt contract (the "still-learning gate set too strict" failure mode, the cross-midnight orphaned-session problem, and the native-feature-scope-creep risk), and lands on a clear build-priority call: nail logging/check-in/recap/gated-insights/paywall first, treat HealthKit/Live Activity/widget as gracefully-degradable. That's consistent with what the prompt contract already decided, so nothing here contradicts it — it just adds texture. Good enough to lock in.

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** People who are tired and curious why, not sleep-tech enthusiasts — most likely folks who tried Sleep Cycle, Apple Health's sleep tracking, or a Whoop/Oura and bounced off the cost, the hardware requirement, or a hypnogram they couldn't act on. The realistic usage pattern is two ~10-second taps a day: one right before bed, one right after waking, frequently done half-asleep. That implies large tap targets, no required typing on the common path, and tolerance for the user abandoning the screen mid-log and returning later without losing state or double-logging.

**Comparable apps or patterns (drawn from general knowledge, not fresh research — labeled as such):**
- *Sleep Cycle* — mic/motion sensing, graph-heavy, subscription-gated trends. Somna's whole pitch is not being this.
- *AutoSleep / Pillow* — assume an Apple Watch is worn; breaks for a meaningful slice of Somna's stated target (students, parents, shift workers who may not own/wear a watch).
- *Streaks / Way of Life* — the habit-streak mechanic Somna's free tier logging leans on.
- *Bearable / Exist.io* — closest conceptual match for "plain-English correlation instead of a graph," just applied to mood/symptoms broadly rather than sleep specifically. Their known failure mode — users quit the daily log before the insight engine has enough data to say anything useful — is exactly the invalidation criteria already named upstream, and should actively shape the build, not just be acknowledged.

**Platform-specific opportunities:**
- Live Activity / Dynamic Island as a low-friction on-ramp into the morning check-in (bedtime countdown flips to a "log your wake-up" tap target) — genuinely a good fit for this app's rhythm, but real ActivityKit footguns exist (activities can silently fail to start, background updates aren't guaranteed to land on time, simulator testing is unreliable — needs a real device to trust). Treat as an enhancement layered on a working in-app flow, not load-bearing.
- WidgetKit's "last night's score" only updates reliably if `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is explicitly called from every write path (logging, check-in) — it won't refresh on its own just because app state changed. This is a concrete implementation requirement, not a detail to leave implicit.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. The "still learning" gate needs a concrete, testable numeric threshold decided now (e.g., minimum nights with a tag present vs. absent, minimum total nights logged) — set it too loose and you get false confidence (the risk already flagged upstream); set it too strict and consistent loggers never see a single insight for weeks, which kills trust just as fast. Both failure directions need guarding against, not just the first.
2. Bedtime and wake are logged as separate taps, possibly on different calendar dates, possibly with one side skipped entirely for a night. The data model and correlation engine must handle orphaned/incomplete sessions across midnight and DST boundaries without silently dropping nights or crashing — this needs explicit handling, not an assumption that logs always arrive in tidy pairs.
3. HealthKit, Live Activity, and WidgetKit are three separate pieces of native surface area that can each absorb disproportionate build time for marginal benefit versus the core loop. Consistent with the prompt contract's "degrade gracefully" call — if time is tight, these are the first things to simplify, not the logging/check-in/insight/paywall loop.

**Implications for the first build:** Build order should prioritize logging → check-in → recap → a correctly-gated insight engine → paywall wiring, in that order of non-negotiability. Because the insight engine only proves itself against real accumulated data, the build should include a way to exercise it against more than zero nights immediately — either a debug-only seed-data path or unit tests against synthetic logs — so the "still learning" threshold and the transition into real insight text can actually be verified during development rather than assumed correct.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Product Research

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:15:34._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Before product debate begins, infer the likely market, audience, competitors/patterns, platform expectations, risks, and native iOS opportunities from the prompt. Label assumptions clearly and turn the research into implications for the first build.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex returned empty output — refusing to fabricate a response. See logs/._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Here's my independent read on this before anyone else weighs in.

The realistic audience is people who already tried Sleep Cycle, Apple's own Health app sleep tracking, or a Whoop/Oura and bounced off either the cost, the hardware, or the fact that the output was a hypnogram nobody could act on. That's the wedge — this isn't competing on sensor accuracy, it's competing on "tells you something you can actually do differently tonight." I'd assume the primary use pattern is two 10-second taps a day, most likely right before bed and right after the alarm, probably half-asleep both times — which has real UI consequences: large tap targets, minimal required fields, no typing required for the common path, and the app needs to survive someone opening it, doing nothing for 20 seconds because they fell back asleep, and reopening it later without losing state or double-logging.

Comparable apps worth learning from, with the caveat that this is my own general knowledge of the category rather than fresh research: Sleep Cycle (mic/motion sensing, graph-heavy, subscription for trends), AutoSleep and Pillow (HealthKit-dependent, assume everyone's wearing an Apple Watch, which breaks for a chunk of the target audience described in this spec), and simpler streak-style habit trackers like Streaks or Way of Life for the check-in/streak mechanic. The gap Somna is explicitly aiming at — plain-English causal-flavored insight instead of a graph — is closer to what Bearable or Exist.io try to do for mood/symptom correlation, just narrower and sleep-specific. The risk with that comparison: those apps are widely known for taking a long time to feel useful and for people abandoning the daily log before the insight engine has enough data to say anything, which is the exact invalidation criteria already flagged upstream. That's not a new risk I'm surfacing, it's the central one, and it should shape the build, not just be acknowledged.

Platform-specific opportunity: this is a genuinely good Live Activity / Dynamic Island use case if it's scoped honestly — a quiet bedtime countdown that turns into "log your wake-up" as a Live Activity tap target the moment a wake time is plausible, is a much lower-friction path into the morning check-in than expecting someone to remember to open the app. That said, I'd treat that as a nice-to-have layered on top of a working in-app flow, not a load-bearing part of the core loop, because ActivityKit has real footguns — activities can silently fail to start if budget/permissions are off, background updates aren't guaranteed to fire on time, and testing it thoroughly in a simulator is limited (you basically need a device to trust it). WidgetKit's timeline refresh has a similar honesty problem: "tonight's target bedtime" on a widget is easy, "last night's score" updates reliably only if the timeline provider is reloaded at the right moments, which won't happen automatically just because the user logged something in the app unless that reload is explicitly triggered (WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines) at every write path.

The biggest risk I'd flag that isn't just a restatement of the spec's own risk section: correlation-of-tiny-N statistics are dangerous even with an honest "still learning" gate, because the gate itself needs a real threshold decision (how many nights, how many nights with each tag present vs absent) and if that threshold is set carelessly you get a different failure mode — the app confidently declares something is "still learning" forever because the bar was set too high, and users who did log consistently for three weeks never see a single insight, which kills trust just as fast as false confidence would. This needs a concrete, testable number decided at build time (something like: minimum 5 nights with a tag present and 5 without, over at least 10 total logged nights, before that specific tag correlation is shown), not a vague "enough data" check.

Second risk: the data model has to gracefully handle the messy real-world logging pattern of bedtime logged one night and wake time logged the next morning as two separate taps, possibly on two different calendar dates, possibly with the user skipping one side entirely for a night. If bedtime/wake are modeled as a single record created at bedtime-log time, the morning check-in flow needs to reliably find "last night's still-open session" across a midnight boundary and DST transition, or you get orphaned records and the correlation engine silently drops nights instead of surfacing an error the user can act on.

Third risk, more of a build-priority one: HealthKit and Live Activity and the widget are three separate pieces of native-platform surface area that can each eat a day of debugging for marginal user-facing benefit compared to just making the core two-tap logging flow and the insight screen excellent. Given "smaller finished app over a larger half-built app" is a stated global rule, my instinct is the first build should nail logging, check-in, recap, and a correctly-gated insight engine with the paywall wired in, and treat HealthKit/Live Activity/widget as the first things cut in scope if time runs short — which the prompt contract already anticipated by calling those "degrade gracefully," and I'd keep that ordering.

For the first build specifically, the implication I want preserved: seed a way to test the correlation engine against more than zero real nights of data without literally waiting three weeks — either a debug-only seed data path or unit tests against synthetic logs — because otherwise nobody building this can verify the "still learning" gate or the insight text actually flips into showing real numbers, and that logic is the entire product differentiator per the spec's own positioning.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex didn't weigh in again this round, but the single Claude pass is thorough, internally consistent, and directly answers everything this phase is supposed to produce — there's no live disagreement to resolve, just one solid take that names its own assumptions. It correctly treats the prior phase's comparable-apps knowledge as informed judgment rather than claiming real market research was done, flags concrete risks beyond what was already in the prompt contract (the "still-learning gate set too strict" failure mode, the cross-midnight orphaned-session problem, and the native-feature-scope-creep risk), and lands on a clear build-priority call: nail logging/check-in/recap/gated-insights/paywall first, treat HealthKit/Live Activity/widget as gracefully-degradable. That's consistent with what the prompt contract already decided, so nothing here contradicts it — it just adds texture. Good enough to lock in.

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** People who are tired and curious why, not sleep-tech enthusiasts — most likely folks who tried Sleep Cycle, Apple Health's sleep tracking, or a Whoop/Oura and bounced off the cost, the hardware requirement, or a hypnogram they couldn't act on. The realistic usage pattern is two ~10-second taps a day: one right before bed, one right after waking, frequently done half-asleep. That implies large tap targets, no required typing on the common path, and tolerance for the user abandoning the screen mid-log and returning later without losing state or double-logging.

**Comparable apps or patterns (drawn from general knowledge, not fresh research — labeled as such):**
- *Sleep Cycle* — mic/motion sensing, graph-heavy, subscription-gated trends. Somna's whole pitch is not being this.
- *AutoSleep / Pillow* — assume an Apple Watch is worn; breaks for a meaningful slice of Somna's stated target (students, parents, shift workers who may not own/wear a watch).
- *Streaks / Way of Life* — the habit-streak mechanic Somna's free tier logging leans on.
- *Bearable / Exist.io* — closest conceptual match for "plain-English correlation instead of a graph," just applied to mood/symptoms broadly rather than sleep specifically. Their known failure mode — users quit the daily log before the insight engine has enough data to say anything useful — is exactly the invalidation criteria already named upstream, and should actively shape the build, not just be acknowledged.

**Platform-specific opportunities:**
- Live Activity / Dynamic Island as a low-friction on-ramp into the morning check-in (bedtime countdown flips to a "log your wake-up" tap target) — genuinely a good fit for this app's rhythm, but real ActivityKit footguns exist (activities can silently fail to start, background updates aren't guaranteed to land on time, simulator testing is unreliable — needs a real device to trust). Treat as an enhancement layered on a working in-app flow, not load-bearing.
- WidgetKit's "last night's score" only updates reliably if `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is explicitly called from every write path (logging, check-in) — it won't refresh on its own just because app state changed. This is a concrete implementation requirement, not a detail to leave implicit.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. The "still learning" gate needs a concrete, testable numeric threshold decided now (e.g., minimum nights with a tag present vs. absent, minimum total nights logged) — set it too loose and you get false confidence (the risk already flagged upstream); set it too strict and consistent loggers never see a single insight for weeks, which kills trust just as fast. Both failure directions need guarding against, not just the first.
2. Bedtime and wake are logged as separate taps, possibly on different calendar dates, possibly with one side skipped entirely for a night. The data model and correlation engine must handle orphaned/incomplete sessions across midnight and DST boundaries without silently dropping nights or crashing — this needs explicit handling, not an assumption that logs always arrive in tidy pairs.
3. HealthKit, Live Activity, and WidgetKit are three separate pieces of native surface area that can each absorb disproportionate build time for marginal benefit versus the core loop. Consistent with the prompt contract's "degrade gracefully" call — if time is tight, these are the first things to simplify, not the logging/check-in/insight/paywall loop.

**Implications for the first build:** Build order should prioritize logging → check-in → recap → a correctly-gated insight engine → paywall wiring, in that order of non-negotiability. Because the insight engine only proves itself against real accumulated data, the build should include a way to exercise it against more than zero nights immediately — either a debug-only seed-data path or unit tests against synthetic logs — so the "still learning" threshold and the transition into real insight text can actually be verified during development rather than assumed correct.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. All five required sections are present and concrete rather than generic: audience/use-context ties directly to the spec's stated target users and derives real UI implications (large tap targets, no required typing, tolerant of abandon-and-resume); comparable apps are correctly labeled as drawn from general knowledge rather than fabricated research, and each comparison yields an actionable takeaway rather than just a name-drop; platform opportunities (Live Activity as check-in on-ramp, WidgetKit's explicit `reloadTimelines` requirement) are specific enough to hand directly to a build phase; three concrete risks are named (still-learning threshold cutting both ways, cross-midnight/DST orphaned-session handling, native-surface-area scope creep) — none are restatements of the prompt contract, each adds new texture; implications for the first build give a clear priority order and a testability requirement (seed data/synthetic logs) so the correlation engine can actually be verified pre-launch.

Nothing here contradicts the locked prompt contract — it reinforces the "Live Activity/widget degrade gracefully" decision rule with concrete technical reasoning (ActivityKit footguns, simulator unreliability) rather than re-litigating it. Ready to lock in; next phase (design/build) has a usable, non-redundant artifact.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex didn't weigh in again this round, but the single Claude pass is thorough, internally consistent, and directly answers everything this phase is supposed to produce — there's no live disagreement to resolve, just one solid take that names its own assumptions. It correctly treats the prior phase's comparable-apps knowledge as informed judgment rather than claiming real market research was done, flags concrete risks beyond what was already in the prompt contract (the "still-learning gate set too strict" failure mode, the cross-midnight orphaned-session problem, and the native-feature-scope-creep risk), and lands on a clear build-priority call: nail logging/check-in/recap/gated-insights/paywall first, treat HealthKit/Live Activity/widget as gracefully-degradable. That's consistent with what the prompt contract already decided, so nothing here contradicts it — it just adds texture. Good enough to lock in.

## Final Output

**Audience and use context:** People who are tired and curious why, not sleep-tech enthusiasts — most likely folks who tried Sleep Cycle, Apple Health's sleep tracking, or a Whoop/Oura and bounced off the cost, the hardware requirement, or a hypnogram they couldn't act on. The realistic usage pattern is two ~10-second taps a day: one right before bed, one right after waking, frequently done half-asleep. That implies large tap targets, no required typing on the common path, and tolerance for the user abandoning the screen mid-log and returning later without losing state or double-logging.

**Comparable apps or patterns (drawn from general knowledge, not fresh research — labeled as such):**
- *Sleep Cycle* — mic/motion sensing, graph-heavy, subscription-gated trends. Somna's whole pitch is not being this.
- *AutoSleep / Pillow* — assume an Apple Watch is worn; breaks for a meaningful slice of Somna's stated target (students, parents, shift workers who may not own/wear a watch).
- *Streaks / Way of Life* — the habit-streak mechanic Somna's free tier logging leans on.
- *Bearable / Exist.io* — closest conceptual match for "plain-English correlation instead of a graph," just applied to mood/symptoms broadly rather than sleep specifically. Their known failure mode — users quit the daily log before the insight engine has enough data to say anything useful — is exactly the invalidation criteria already named upstream, and should actively shape the build, not just be acknowledged.

- Live Activity / Dynamic Island as a low-friction on-ramp into the morning check-in (bedtime countdown flips to a "log your wake-up" tap target) — genuinely a good fit for this app's rhythm, but real ActivityKit footguns exist (activities can silently fail to start, background updates aren't guaranteed to land on time, simulator testing is unreliable — needs a real device to trust). Treat as an enhancement layered on a working in-app flow, not load-bearing.
- WidgetKit's "last night's score" only updates reliably if `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is explicitly called from every write path (logging, check-in) — it won't refresh on its own just because app state changed. This is a concrete implementation requirement, not a detail to leave implicit.

**Major assumptions and risks:**
1. The "still learning" gate needs a concrete, testable numeric threshold decided now (e.g., minimum nights with a tag present vs. absent, minimum total nights logged) — set it too loose and you get false confidence (the risk already flagged upstream); set it too strict and consistent loggers never see a single insight for weeks, which kills trust just as fast. Both failure directions need guarding against, not just the first.
2. Bedtime and wake are logged as separate taps, possibly on different calendar dates, possibly with one side skipped entirely for a night. 3. HealthKit, Live Activity, and WidgetKit are three separate pieces of native surface area that can each absorb disproportionate build time for marginal benefit versus the core loop. Consistent with the prompt contract's "degrade gracefully" call — if time is tight, these are the first things to simplify, not the logging/check-in/insight/paywall loop.

phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Portfolio Selection

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:16:51._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

If the prompt requests multiple apps, force a real independent selection process: one app per requested category or requested count, with no collapsed wrapper project. Produce the required portfolio-json manifest for sibling child projects. If the prompt is a single app, explicitly record that no portfolio split is needed.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Product Strategist · User Advocate) — Round 1**

This is a single-app run, not a portfolio parent. The parent portfolio prompt talks about picking 5 concepts, but that selection already happened upstream — this child project's job is narrower: build the one app it was handed, Somna, and nothing else. There's no ambiguity here worth debating; I'll flag it plainly so nobody in this room starts second-guessing whether Somna is the "right" pick among 10 candidates. It isn't this phase's call to make.

If I put myself in the shoes of the actual user this app is for — say, a nursing student who wakes up groggy three days a week and has already deleted Sleep Cycle because the hypnogram meant nothing to her — the single most important design boundary isn't a feature, it's restraint. The whole promise collapses if the two daily taps (bedtime log, morning check-in) feel like homework. Every workflow beyond those two taps — HealthKit import, Live Activity, widget — is in service of making the core loop easier or more visible, never a replacement for it, and never allowed to add friction to it. If HealthKit permission prompts show up unprompted, or the widget nags her to open the app, that's a direct violation of the target user's tolerance level, and I'd reject any build direction that adds a single unnecessary tap or screen to the nightly/morning ritual.

The core problem this app solves is causal confusion, not tracking. She doesn't need a log of her sleep — she has that instinct already ("I feel bad"). She needs the app to notice patterns she can't hold in her head across weeks: that the nights she has late coffee track with worse mornings, say. That means the correlation engine is the actual product, and everything else — logging, recap, streaks — exists to feed it data. If I had to cut scope under time pressure, I'd protect logging → check-in → recap → correlation engine → paywall in that order, exactly as the prior phase already concluded, and I'd cut Live Activity and widget before I'd cut anything in that core chain. I want that priority order preserved verbatim in the final output, not softened.

The single biggest risk to my own stance: "restraint" is easy to say and hard to enforce once a build phase starts adding "just one more tag chip" or "just one more onboarding screen" because it seems harmless in isolation. Each of those individually seems fine; collectively they turn a 10-second check-in into a 45-second one, which is exactly the failure mode the spec's own invalidation criteria warns about (users keep the bedtime log, drop the morning check-in by week 2). So the thing I want the final output to preserve concretely: the morning check-in's default/fast path must be completable with taps only — no required text entry, no more than one screen, optional tags collapsed/hidden behind a single "add detail" affordance rather than shown by default. If a later build phase ships a check-in flow that takes more than ~3 taps for the common case, that's a product failure even if it technically satisfies "morning check-in with optional tags" as written.

Second thing I want carried forward: the "still learning" state has to read as encouraging, not clinical or apologetic. A user who's logged 4 nights should see something like progress toward a goal ("3 more nights until your first insight") rather than a bare "not enough data" message — that's the difference between an honest partial state and a discouraging dead end, and it directly affects whether she comes back on day 5.

No portfolio-json needed here — this is a single selected app (somna) inheriting a build=true decision made at the parent level. The output for this phase should just state plainly: single-app run, no child projects, build proceeds directly on Somna per the locked prompt contract and research above, with the core-loop-first priority and the check-in friction ceiling as the two things I most want protected against dilution in later phases.

phase_discussions.title: Initial Discussion
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Only Claude showed up this round (Codex is still down), but the pass is a genuinely complete answer to what this phase needs, and it doesn't fight anything already locked in the prompt contract, product research, or portfolio selection — it sharpens those into concrete IA and data-model decisions. The one new thing worth calling out: it reframes Somna as "an insight app with lightweight logging as the mechanism" rather than "a tracker with insights bolted on" — a framing shift, not a scope change, and it adds real teeth to two things the spec left vague: how bedtime/wake events get stitched into a "night" (a plausibility-window rule, keyed by timestamp not calendar date, so naps and skipped nights don't corrupt the model), and a third Insights-screen state ("logged enough, but this tag has no signal") that the spec's own language would have let a builder skip. Nothing here is contested — it's ready to lock.

## Final Output

**One-sentence product promise:** Somna turns two ten-second daily logs into a plain-English answer for why you feel rested or wrecked — the insight is the product, the logging is just fuel for it.

**Primary user and scenario:** Someone tired and curious, not a sleep-tech enthusiast — a student, parent, or shift worker who's bounced off Sleep Cycle/Whoop-style apps because the output was a graph nobody could act on. They interact with Somna in two near-unconscious ten-second bursts a day (tapping bedtime around 11pm, tapping wake + a quick mood/energy check-in around 7am), and check in on trends maybe once a week. The app has to survive being opened, abandoned mid-log, and reopened later without losing state or double-logging.

**Core loop:** log bedtime → (sleep happens) → log wake + morning check-in → weekly recap surfaces → correlation insights update → an insight subtly changes tonight's behavior → log bedtime again. The loop only has value if the last step closes back into the first — e.g., an insight like "you feel better with 7+ hours" should be visible from the bedtime-logging screen itself (a suggested target bedtime), not stranded on a separate Insights tab.

**IA shape:** Two modes, not one dashboard trying to do both — a "fast capture" mode (bedtime/wake/check-in) that's reachable in one tap with no navigation, and a slower "reflection" mode (recap, insights, history). The capture surface should visually collapse to a quiet "done for today" state once nothing's actionable, rather than staying a live dashboard demanding attention.

**Hard scope boundaries (new, on top of what's already locked):**
- No free-text notes field in v1 — it fights the sub-3-tap fast path and can't be correlated by a rule-based engine anyway. Tags only, from a small fixed set.
- Sessions are keyed by their own start/end timestamps, not calendar date, so odd-hours logging (shift workers, naps) doesn't corrupt pairing — naps themselves aren't a feature to build, just something the model must not choke on.
- A wake log only auto-pairs with an open bedtime log if the gap is under a plausibility ceiling (~16 hours); otherwise the dangling bedtime closes out as "incomplete/no wake logged" and the new wake log starts its own orphan session. This is a real rule in the data model, not implicit behavior.
- The Insights screen needs three states, not two: "still learning" (below threshold, encouraging progress), "here's what we found" (threshold met), and "no signal for this tag" (enough nights logged, but no contrast group exists for that specific tag — e.g., always drinks coffee). Conflating the third into the first reads as the app being permanently stuck at 0%.

**Measurable success criteria:**
1. Cold-start bedtime-log-to-morning-check-in cycle completes in under 15 seconds combined, no required text field.
2. With zero data, every screen (Today, Insights, History, Recap) shows a real designed empty state — no blank views, no crashes.
3. The correlation engine produces zero insights below its sample-size threshold and at least one correctly-worded insight once seeded/synthetic data crosses it — verifiable without waiting three real weeks.
4. Out-of-order or incomplete logs (wake with no bedtime, bedtime with no wake for 3+ nights) never crash the app or corrupt the next night's pairing.

This carries forward everything already locked (core-loop build priority, sub-3-tap check-in, encouraging "still learning" copy, Live Activity/widget degrade-gracefully) and adds the session-pairing rule and third Insights state as concrete requirements for the build phase.

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:

Output directory:

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```
