```
UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Deeply analyze requirements, edge cases, constraints, assumptions, risks, and open questions, resolving ambiguity left from earlier phases.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Skeptic) — Round 1**

Coming at this round as the skeptic — my job here is to find what still quietly breaks even after four rounds of consensus, not to restate what's already locked.

Start with the correlation engine, because I think it has a subtler problem than "sample size threshold" that nobody's named yet: confounding. If someone reliably exercises on the same days they also have low screen time and good sleep, a rule-based engine comparing single tags against energy will happily report "screen time correlates with energy" when the real driver could be exercise, or vice versa — and it can't tell the difference because it's not controlling for co-occurring tags. This isn't a threshold problem, it's a structural limitation of a simple rule-based approach, and I think it needs to be handled honestly rather than engineered away: insight copy should never use causal language ("you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours" reads causal even though it's really "nights with 7+ hours logged X higher average energy than nights with less"). I'd insist the actual shipped copy stays strictly associational — "on nights you slept 7+ hours, your energy averaged X% higher" — both because it's honest and because Apple's App Store review has real teeth around health-adjacent claims that sound diagnostic or medical. This app should also carry a one-line non-medical disclaimer somewhere reachable (settings or first-run), because "tells you what actually helps you feel rested" is exactly the kind of phrasing that invites App Review scrutiny if the in-app copy tips into claiming causation or medical benefit.

Second cluster of edge cases around the data model that I don't think prior rounds fully closed out: what happens when the user taps "log bedtime" a second time while a session is already open — does that silently create a second open session that later corrupts pairing, or does it edit the existing one? I'd resolve this now: a second bedtime tap while one session is open should update the existing open session's start time, never create a second concurrent open session. Similarly, can a morning check-in exist without any paired bedtime session at all — say, someone installs the app and wants to log how they feel today without ever having logged last night's bedtime? I think the answer has to be yes, check-ins should be able to exist standalone (they just won't feed duration-based correlation until they're paired), otherwise the fast path breaks for anyone who missed a step, which is exactly the kind of brittleness that causes the invalidation criteria's feared drop-off. And what about someone editing or deleting a past log entirely — is that even allowed in v1? If it is, and it changes a night that already contributed to a shown insight, the insight text can change or disappear on the next screen visit with no explanation, which will read as a bug even if it's technically correct. My resolution: allow edit of only the most-recent/open session (fixing a fat-fingered time) and allow deletion of any entry, but treat every computed correlation as fully recomputed on each app foreground rather than cached — that keeps the system honest instead of trying to diff and explain why an insight changed.

Third: streaks and multiple-check-ins-per-day for shift workers. The product brief already commits the streak to react specifically to the morning check-in, but shift workers and nappers can plausibly generate two check-ins in one calendar day (an odd-hours "wake" plus a genuine nap-wake later). If the streak logic counts per check-in rather than per calendar day, that's an easy exploit/bug where someone can farm streak days; if it's naively per-calendar-day, the second same-day check-in needs to be a no-op or overwrite rather than silently ignored (which would feel broken) or silently double-counted (which would feel gamed). I'd lock this as: streak increments at most once per local calendar day, keyed to the check-in timestamp, and a second same-day check-in updates the existing day's record rather than creating a new streak tick.

Fourth, something genuinely new: privacy leakage through the lock screen. A Live Activity bedtime countdown and a widget showing "last night's score" both render content on the lock screen where anyone glancing at the phone — a roommate, partner, kid — can see it. Mood/energy scores or specific insight text on a lock-screen surface is a real disclosure risk for a category this personal. I'd want the lock-screen-visible surfaces kept deliberately generic (a countdown timer, a bedtime target time) and never show the mood/energy number or insight text there, even though it's convenient — this is a case where "degrade gracefully" should mean "degrade toward privacy," not just toward simplicity.

Fifth, accessibility risk specific to this app's stated design: the mood/energy check-in is described as "a single tap-driven scale," which strongly implies a custom control rather than a stock SwiftUI `Slider` — and custom scale/dial controls are one of the most commonly broken VoiceOver patterns because they don't announce values or support the adjustable trait unless explicitly built to. I want this named explicitly rather than assumed handled by "accessibility is a product requirement" boilerplate: the check-in control needs explicit accessibility value/adjustable-action support tested with VoiceOver, not just large tap targets. Same concern, smaller, for the gradient backgrounds — indigo-to-violet gradients under white text are a classic WCAG-contrast failure zone depending on where on the gradient the text sits; text should sit on a card surface with a guaranteed-contrast fill, not directly on the raw background gradient.

Sixth: clock and calendar honesty. DST fallback (a 25-hour day) or a user manually changing device time backward could produce a computed sleep duration that's negative or absurd. The 16-hour plausibility window already agreed handles the "too long" case for pairing, but I don't think it explicitly handles a negative duration (wake timestamp earlier than bedtime timestamp because of a clock change) — that needs its own guard that discards or flags the session as implausible rather than displaying "-2h 14m" or feeding a negative number into the correlation engine.

Seventh, a data-loss risk that's real even though it's technically consistent with the locked non-goals: there is no cloud sync in v1, full stop, which means losing or resetting the phone loses months of sleep history permanently, with the user never having been warned that's the deal. I'm not proposing we build export or sync now — that would contradict what's already locked — but I want it named plainly as a residual risk with a named mitigation: the local SwiftData store should be left under standard iOS device-backup behavior (i.e., not excluded from iCloud/iTunes backup) as a deliberate decision, since that's the only backstop this version gets, and that should be a stated choice rather than an accidental default nobody decided on.

Last, StoreKit failure modes: if the product list fails to load (offline at first launch, sandbox hiccup) or the entitlement check hangs, the free-tier core loop must never block waiting on that — entitlement resolution has to fail open to "treat as free tier" rather than fail closed or spin, since a paywall check must never be capable of preventing someone from logging bedtime.

Final assumptions I'd lock going into design: iPhone-only layout for v1; no push notifications/local reminders in this build (Live Activity is the only proactive nudge); check-ins can exist unpaired; edits limited to the most-recent open session with deletion otherwise, correlations always recomputed fresh rather than cached and diffed; streak increments at most once per local calendar day; no analytics/telemetry SDK given the local-first privacy stance; and insight copy is contractually associational, never causal, with a one-line non-medical disclaimer present somewhere in the app.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice showed up this round again (Codex still unavailable), but it's a genuinely thorough skeptic pass that doesn't just restate prior phases — it closes real gaps that were still open after four rounds of "logging → check-in → recap → correlation → paywall" agreement. Nothing here contradicts anything locked earlier; it resolves ambiguity the earlier phases left soft (correlation causality language, duplicate-tap handling, edit/delete semantics, streak-farming, lock-screen privacy, accessibility of a custom scale control, clock/DST edge cases, backup posture, StoreKit fail-open behavior). This is exactly what the detailed_discussion phase exists to do, and it's specific and testable enough to hand straight to design/build.

## Final Output

**Resolved requirements (new this round, on top of everything already locked):**
- Insight copy must be strictly associational, never causal ("on nights you slept 7+ hours, your energy averaged X% higher" — not "sleeping 7+ hours makes you X% more rested"). A one-line non-medical disclaimer must be reachable (settings or first-run).
- A second "log bedtime" tap while a session is already open updates that session's start time — it never creates a second concurrent open session.
- Morning check-ins can exist standalone, unpaired to any bedtime session (they just don't feed duration-based correlation until paired) — this keeps the fast path alive for anyone who missed a step.
- Editing is limited to the most-recent/open session (fixing a fat-fingered time); deletion is allowed on any entry. Correlations are always recomputed fresh on each app foreground, never cached-and-diffed, so there's no need to explain why a shown insight silently changed.
- Streaks increment at most once per local calendar day, keyed to the check-in timestamp; a second same-day check-in updates that day's record rather than creating a new tick or being silently dropped.
- Lock-screen-visible surfaces (Live Activity, widget) show only generic content — a countdown or a target bedtime — never mood/energy scores or insight text. Privacy is the default to degrade toward, not just simplicity.
- The mood/energy check-in control (a custom tap-driven scale, not a stock Slider) needs explicit VoiceOver value/adjustable-trait support, tested directly — not assumed covered by generic "accessibility is a requirement" language. Card text sits on a guaranteed-contrast surface fill, never directly on the raw gradient.
- Entitlement/StoreKit checks fail open to free tier — a slow or failed product-list load or entitlement check can never block or delay the core logging loop.

**Edge cases enumerated:**
- Duplicate/overlapping bedtime taps; standalone check-ins with no paired session; editing/deleting past entries that already fed a shown insight; two same-day check-ins from shift-worker/nap patterns; negative or absurd computed durations from DST fallback or manual clock changes (must be discarded/flagged, never displayed or fed to the engine); StoreKit product load failure or hung entitlement check at first launch.

**Data and privacy implications:**
- Confounded tags (e.g., exercise and low screen time co-occurring) mean the rule-based engine can misattribute correlation — mitigated by keeping all copy associational rather than causal, not by trying to engineer true causal inference.
- No cloud sync in v1 is already locked, but that means a lost/reset phone permanently loses history with no warning ever given — mitigated by a deliberate, stated decision to leave the SwiftData store under normal iOS device-backup behavior (not excluded from iCloud/iTunes backup) as the only backstop this version gets.
- No analytics/telemetry SDK, consistent with the local-first privacy stance already sold as part of the product's trust proposition.

**Risk register:**
1. Rule-based correlation confusable with causation → mitigated by strictly associational copy + non-medical disclaimer.
2. Duplicate-session / duplicate-check-in bugs corrupting pairing or streaks → mitigated by the concrete resolution rules above (update-in-place, once-per-day streak ticks).
3. Lock-screen disclosure of personal mood/health data → mitigated by keeping ActivityKit/widget content generic, never scores or insight text.
4. Custom scale control silently breaking VoiceOver → named explicitly as a build/test requirement, not assumed handled.
5. DST/clock-change producing nonsensical durations → guarded by discarding implausible (negative or absurd) sessions rather than displaying or computing on them.
6. Total data loss on device loss/reset with v1's no-sync scope → accepted as a known limitation, mitigated only by leaving standard device backup enabled (a stated choice, not a default nobody made).
7. StoreKit failure blocking the free core loop → mitigated by fail-open entitlement resolution.

**Final assumptions locked for design/build:**
iPhone-only layout for v1; no push notifications/local reminders (Live Activity is the only proactive nudge); check-ins can exist unpaired; edits limited to the most-recent open session, deletion otherwise allowed, correlations always recomputed fresh; streaks increment at most once per local calendar day; no analytics/telemetry SDK; insight copy is contractually associational, never causal, with a reachable one-line non-medical disclaimer; standard iOS backup left enabled as the only data-loss backstop in v1.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. This round's skeptic pass genuinely advances the phase rather than restating prior consensus — it surfaces and resolves seven distinct issues that were previously open gaps (causal-vs-associational insight copy plus App Review exposure, duplicate-session/duplicate-check-in handling, standalone check-ins, edit/delete semantics against already-shown insights, streak-farming via multiple same-day check-ins, lock-screen privacy disclosure, VoiceOver support for a custom scale control, DST/negative-duration guarding, backup posture as a deliberate choice, and StoreKit fail-open behavior).

All required output sections are present and concrete:
- **Resolved requirements**: eight specific, testable rules, each closing a real ambiguity rather than restating locked decisions.
- **Edge cases**: six enumerated cases, each tied to a resolution above.
- **Data and privacy implications**: confounding/causality framing, no-sync data-loss posture, no-telemetry stance — all specific to this app's domain, not generic.
- **Risk register**: seven risks, each with either a concrete mitigation or an explicit "named limitation" (the data-loss-on-reset item correctly uses the "accepted limitation" escape hatch rather than pretending it's solved).
- **Final assumptions**: a tight, buildable list (iPhone-only, no notifications, unpaired check-ins allowed, edit/delete scope, streak keying, no telemetry, associational-only copy, backup posture).

Nothing here contradicts prompt_contract, product_research, initial_discussion, or per_app_product_brief — it composes with them (e.g., the associational-copy rule sharpens the spec's own causal-sounding App Store positioning language without contradicting the locked promise). Acceptance checks satisfied: no major ambiguity remains for design/architecture, and every risk has either a concrete mitigation or is named as an explicit limitation. Ready to close and hand to design/build.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice showed up this round again (Codex still unavailable), but it's a genuinely thorough skeptic pass that doesn't just restate prior phases — it closes real gaps that were still open after four rounds of "logging → check-in → recap → correlation → paywall" agreement. Nothing here contradicts anything locked earlier; it resolves ambiguity the earlier phases left soft (correlation causality language, duplicate-tap handling, edit/delete semantics, streak-farming, lock-screen privacy, accessibility of a custom scale control, clock/DST edge cases, backup posture, StoreKit fail-open behavior). - The mood/energy check-in control (a custom tap-driven scale, not a stock Slider) needs explicit VoiceOver value/adjustable-trait support, tested directly — not assumed covered by generic "accessibility is a requirement" language. phase_discussions.title: App Features
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Codex was unavailable again this round, so it's a single voice, but the pass is thorough and doesn't contradict anything locked in the five prior phases — it just converts all that accumulated detail into a clean MoSCoW list with real acceptance criteria attached to each must-have. The key judgment call — keeping HealthKit/Live Activity/WidgetKit as should-haves rather than musts despite the spec's own "v1 Build Scope" wording — isn't new; it's the same "degrade gracefully" resolution that's been reaffirmed in every phase since next_steps_small. Nothing here is in dispute.

## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each ships with a user story + acceptance criteria):

1. **Bedtime logging** — User can start tonight's sleep session with one tap, time defaulting to now, adjustable via picker.
   - *Acceptance:* a second bedtime tap while a session is open updates that session's start time rather than creating a concurrent second session; sessions are keyed by their own timestamp, not calendar date.

2. **Wake + morning check-in** — User logs wake time and a mood/energy check-in on one screen, ≤3 taps for the fast path, tags optional and collapsed behind an "add detail" affordance.
   - *Acceptance:* submittable with zero tags/zero text; a wake log auto-pairs to an open bedtime session only if the gap is under 16 hours, otherwise the bedtime session closes as incomplete and the wake log becomes its own orphan; a standalone check-in with no paired session is valid, not an error.

3. **Today view** — Shows the single next action (log bedtime, or log wake+check-in) and collapses to a quiet "done for today" state once both are logged.
   - *Acceptance:* correct empty state at first launch with zero data; correct done state same-day after both logs land; never blank or crashes regardless of pairing state.

4. **Weekly recap card** — Summarizes the last 7 days (average sleep, best night, check-in completion rate) with ShareLink.
   - *Acceptance:* renders correctly with fewer than 7 nights of data; shareable output is understandable by a stranger in under 3 seconds with no app context; never gated behind the paywall.

5. **Correlation insights** (narrow scope: sleep-duration-vs-energy, one tag-vs-energy) — Plain-English insights once enough nights are logged.
   - *Acceptance:* a build-time test force-feeds threshold-minus-one nights and asserts zero scored insights, then threshold nights and asserts at least one; all three states (still learning / real insight / no-signal-for-tag) are reachable on demand against test data; copy is associational only, never causal, with the disclaimer reachable; a foreground recompute is always fresh, never a stale cached number.

6. **Data integrity guards** — Sessions can't be corrupted by edge-case input.
   - *Acceptance:* negative/implausible durations (DST, manual clock changes) are discarded, never displayed or fed to the engine; streaks increment at most once per local calendar day keyed to check-in timestamp, with a second same-day check-in updating rather than double-counting or being dropped; edits limited to the most-recent/open session, deletion allowed on any entry.

7. **Somna+ paywall (StoreKit)** — User can see what's locked and purchase it; free-tier logging is never blocked by entitlement checks.
   - *Acceptance:* product-list load failure or a hung entitlement check fails open to free tier with no blocking spinner on Today; Restore Purchases present; only the per-tag correlation view is gated — the coarse duration-vs-energy comparison and the recap card are not.

**Should-have list:** HealthKit read-only duration import (must fully no-op with zero permission granted, never a blocking prompt on first launch); WidgetKit last-night's-score widget (must call `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` from every write path or it'll show stale data, which is worse than no widget); Live Activity bedtime countdown (lock-screen content generic only — never a score or insight text).

**Could-have list:** onboarding walkthrough beyond a single first-run screen; settings polish beyond the required disclaimer/data-management basics; additional tag types beyond the fixed starter set; a richer history-browsing UI beyond a simple list.

**Won't-build:** free-text notes field; any social/friend-comparison layer; push notifications or local reminders; cloud sync/CloudKit in this build; sleep-stage detection or audio recording; causal-language insight copy; any analytics/telemetry SDK.

**One acceptance criterion the group wants preserved word-for-word into the build phase:** the correlation engine's threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) is a must-have test, not optional polish — it's the only proof this app didn't ship a set of hardcoded strings dressed up as an insight engine.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — App Features

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:24:40._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Enumerate and prioritize the full feature set with an explicit scoring / MoSCoW model and clear in-scope vs out-of-scope lines.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this as the person who has to decide what's actually shippable and what breaks under the edge cases we've already enumerated across five prior rounds — the risk here isn't inventing new scope, it's letting the must-have list quietly bloat because everything upstream sounds important. My job this round is to draw hard MoSCoW lines and make sure every must-have has a testable acceptance criterion that maps to a real failure mode, not just a feature description.

Must-haves, and I'm going to state each with the user story and the acceptance criteria that actually catches the bugs we already identified:

Bedtime logging — as a user, I can tap once to start tonight's sleep session, with the time defaulting to now and adjustable via a picker. Acceptance: a second bedtime tap while a session is already open updates the existing open session's start time rather than creating a second concurrent session; the session is stored keyed by its own timestamp, not calendar date.

Wake + morning check-in — as a user, I can log wake time and a mood/energy check-in in one screen, completable in the fast path with three taps or fewer, tags optional and collapsed behind a single "add detail" affordance. Acceptance: the check-in can be submitted with zero tags and zero required text; a wake log auto-pairs to an open bedtime session only if the gap is under 16 hours, otherwise the bedtime session closes as incomplete and the wake log starts an orphan; a standalone check-in with no paired bedtime session is a valid state, not an error.

Today view — as a user, I see one screen that tells me what to do next (log bedtime, or log wake+check-in) and collapses to a quiet "done for today" state once both are logged. Acceptance: correct empty state on first launch with zero data; correct done state same-day after both logs land; this view never shows a crash or blank screen regardless of pairing state.

Weekly recap card — as a user, I see a card summarizing the last 7 days (average sleep, best night, check-in completion) and can share it. Acceptance: renders correctly with fewer than 7 nights of data (partial week, not broken week); ShareLink produces an image/text a stranger could understand in under 3 seconds without app context; recap is never behind the paywall.

Correlation insights (narrow: duration-vs-energy, one tag-vs-energy) — as a user, I see plain-English insights once I've logged enough nights. Acceptance: this is the one I'd be strictest on — a build-time test must force-feed exactly threshold-minus-one nights and assert zero scored insights render, then exactly threshold nights and assert at least one renders; the screen supports all three states on demand against test data (still learning with encouraging copy, real insight, no-signal-for-this-tag); insight copy is associational only, never causal, with the disclaimer reachable; edits/deletes trigger a full recompute on next foreground rather than a stale cached number.

Data integrity guards — as a user, my sessions never get corrupted by edge-case input. Acceptance: negative or implausible durations from DST or manual clock changes are discarded, never displayed or fed into the engine; streak increments at most once per local calendar day keyed to check-in timestamp, and a second same-day check-in updates rather than double-counts or gets dropped; editing is allowed only on the most-recent/open session, deletion allowed on any entry.

Somna+ paywall (StoreKit) — as a user, I can see what's locked and purchase it, and the app never blocks my free-tier logging while checking entitlement. Acceptance: product-list load failure or a hung entitlement check fails open to free tier with no spinner blocking the Today view; Restore Purchases is present; correlation engine's per-tag view is gated, the coarse duration-vs-energy comparison and the recap card are not.

I'd hold the line that HealthKit, Live Activity, and WidgetKit are should-haves this round, not musts, even though the top-level spec lists them under v1 scope — that's consistent with every prior phase's "degrade gracefully" call, and from a pure risk standpoint these are the three features most likely to eat unbounded time for marginal payoff (ActivityKit's flaky simulator behavior, widget timeline reload discipline, HealthKit permission-denial paths) without touching whether the core promise works. Should-list: HealthKit read-only duration import (must fully no-op with zero permission granted, never a blocking prompt on first launch), WidgetKit last-night's-score widget (must call `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` from every write path or it'll show stale data forever, which is worse than no widget), Live Activity bedtime countdown (lock-screen content generic only — never a score or insight text, per the privacy rule already locked).

Could-list: onboarding walkthrough beyond a single first-run screen, settings screen polish beyond the required disclaimer/data-management basics, additional tag types beyond the fixed starter set, history browsing UI beyond a simple list.

Won't-build, and I want this explicit so nobody tries to sneak it back in under time pressure: free-text notes field, any social/friend-comparison layer, push notifications or local reminders, cloud sync/CloudKit in this build, sleep-stage detection or audio recording of any kind, causal-language insight copy, analytics/telemetry SDK of any kind.

The biggest risk to my own list: I'm putting Live Activity and widget below the line even though the original spec's "v1 Build Scope" line literally names them, and someone could push back that this contradicts the spec. I'd defend it on the grounds that this exact tradeoff was already argued and locked in the next_steps_small phase and reaffirmed in every phase since — "degrade gracefully" was the explicit resolution, not something I'm introducing now. What I don't want to see happen is that language getting reinterpreted at build time as "nice UI polish" rather than "acceptable to ship without if time runs out." The one thing I want the final phase output to preserve word-for-word: the correlation-engine's threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) is a must-have acceptance criterion, not a should — it's the single test that proves this app is honest about the one thing its entire pitch depends on, and if a build phase skips writing that test, we have no way of knowing whether we shipped a real insight engine or a set of hardcoded strings with a data table behind them.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex was unavailable again this round, so it's a single voice, but the pass is thorough and doesn't contradict anything locked in the five prior phases — it just converts all that accumulated detail into a clean MoSCoW list with real acceptance criteria attached to each must-have. The key judgment call — keeping HealthKit/Live Activity/WidgetKit as should-haves rather than musts despite the spec's own "v1 Build Scope" wording — isn't new; it's the same "degrade gracefully" resolution that's been reaffirmed in every phase since next_steps_small. Nothing here is in dispute.

## Final Output

**Must-have features** (each ships with a user story + acceptance criteria):

1. **Bedtime logging** — User can start tonight's sleep session with one tap, time defaulting to now, adjustable via picker.
   - *Acceptance:* a second bedtime tap while a session is open updates that session's start time rather than creating a concurrent second session; sessions are keyed by their own timestamp, not calendar date.

2. **Wake + morning check-in** — User logs wake time and a mood/energy check-in on one screen, ≤3 taps for the fast path, tags optional and collapsed behind an "add detail" affordance.
   - *Acceptance:* submittable with zero tags/zero text; a wake log auto-pairs to an open bedtime session only if the gap is under 16 hours, otherwise the bedtime session closes as incomplete and the wake log becomes its own orphan; a standalone check-in with no paired session is valid, not an error.

3. **Today view** — Shows the single next action (log bedtime, or log wake+check-in) and collapses to a quiet "done for today" state once both are logged.
   - *Acceptance:* correct empty state at first launch with zero data; correct done state same-day after both logs land; never blank or crashes regardless of pairing state.

4. **Weekly recap card** — Summarizes the last 7 days (average sleep, best night, check-in completion rate) with ShareLink.
   - *Acceptance:* renders correctly with fewer than 7 nights of data; shareable output is understandable by a stranger in under 3 seconds with no app context; never gated behind the paywall.

5. **Correlation insights** (narrow scope: sleep-duration-vs-energy, one tag-vs-energy) — Plain-English insights once enough nights are logged.
   - *Acceptance:* a build-time test force-feeds threshold-minus-one nights and asserts zero scored insights, then threshold nights and asserts at least one; all three states (still learning / real insight / no-signal-for-tag) are reachable on demand against test data; copy is associational only, never causal, with the disclaimer reachable; a foreground recompute is always fresh, never a stale cached number.

6. **Data integrity guards** — Sessions can't be corrupted by edge-case input.
   - *Acceptance:* negative/implausible durations (DST, manual clock changes) are discarded, never displayed or fed to the engine; streaks increment at most once per local calendar day keyed to check-in timestamp, with a second same-day check-in updating rather than double-counting or being dropped; edits limited to the most-recent/open session, deletion allowed on any entry.

7. **Somna+ paywall (StoreKit)** — User can see what's locked and purchase it; free-tier logging is never blocked by entitlement checks.
   - *Acceptance:* product-list load failure or a hung entitlement check fails open to free tier with no blocking spinner on Today; Restore Purchases present; only the per-tag correlation view is gated — the coarse duration-vs-energy comparison and the recap card are not.

**Should-have list:** HealthKit read-only duration import (must fully no-op with zero permission granted, never a blocking prompt on first launch); WidgetKit last-night's-score widget (must call `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` from every write path or it'll show stale data, which is worse than no widget); Live Activity bedtime countdown (lock-screen content generic only — never a score or insight text).

**Could-have list:** onboarding walkthrough beyond a single first-run screen; settings polish beyond the required disclaimer/data-management basics; additional tag types beyond the fixed starter set; a richer history-browsing UI beyond a simple list.

**Won't-build:** free-text notes field; any social/friend-comparison layer; push notifications or local reminders; cloud sync/CloudKit in this build; sleep-stage detection or audio recording; causal-language insight copy; any analytics/telemetry SDK.

**One acceptance criterion the group wants preserved word-for-word into the build phase:** the correlation engine's threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) is a must-have test, not optional polish — it's the only proof this app didn't ship a set of hardcoded strings dressed up as an insight engine.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. All four required outputs are present and well-formed:

- **Must-have list**: 7 features, each with a user story and concrete, testable acceptance criteria tied to specific failure modes already identified in prior phases (duplicate-session handling, 16-hour pairing window, threshold-gating test, streak-farming guard, fail-open StoreKit). This isn't generic feature description — each acceptance criterion maps to a real edge case enumerated in `detailed_discussion`.
- **Should-have list**: HealthKit/Live Activity/WidgetKit, correctly demoted from the spec's literal "v1 Build Scope" wording per the "degrade gracefully" resolution locked since `next_steps_small` — this is a reaffirmation of standing consensus, not a new unilateral cut, and it's defended explicitly in the coordinator's reasoning rather than asserted.
- **Could-have list**: reasonable, low-stakes polish items (onboarding, settings polish, extra tags, richer history UI).
- **Won't-build list**: explicit and consistent with every prior phase's non-goals (no free text, no social layer, no push notifications, no cloud sync, no causal copy, no telemetry).

Acceptance checks satisfied: every must-have traces directly to the product promise (logging → check-in → recap → gated insights → paywall, in that order), and the build phase can complete this list — it's narrower than the full spec by design (two insight types, not five; should-have native features can degrade), consistent with "prefer a smaller finished app over a larger half-built app."

No contradiction with any prior locked phase. The one flagged tension (should-have status for spec-named v1 features) is pre-resolved by five rounds of prior consensus, and the coordinator correctly cites that lineage rather than re-litigating it. The closing note pinning the threshold-gating test as non-negotiable is a good concrete carry-forward for the build phase. Ready to close.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Codex was unavailable again this round, so it's a single voice, but the pass is thorough and doesn't contradict anything locked in the five prior phases — it just converts all that accumulated detail into a clean MoSCoW list with real acceptance criteria attached to each must-have. The key judgment call — keeping HealthKit/Live Activity/WidgetKit as should-haves rather than musts despite the spec's own "v1 Build Scope" wording — isn't new; it's the same "degrade gracefully" resolution that's been reaffirmed in every phase since next_steps_small. Nothing here is in dispute.

5. **Correlation insights** (narrow scope: sleep-duration-vs-energy, one tag-vs-energy) — Plain-English insights once enough nights are logged.

phase_discussions.title: Design Discussion
phase_discussions.output:
Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, concrete pass that hits every required output for this phase and doesn't contradict anything locked in the six prior phases — it just turns all that accumulated detail into an actual screen-by-screen design. The big calls: three tabs only (Today/Insights/History, Settings tucked behind a gear icon rather than eating tab real estate), Today as a single mutating-state view rather than four separate screens, bedtime/wake-checkin as sheets rather than pushed screens, a real time-of-day-driven gradient system with fixed bands (not just "dark mode"), and Insights rendered as three genuinely distinct visual compositions rather than one layout with swapped text. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it explicitly protects the two details this whole discussion history has been fighting for — the "done" state visibly reacting to the morning check-in specifically, and the no-signal-for-tag card looking different from still-learning, not just reworded. Good enough to lock.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:**
- **Today** (tab 1, always the launch screen) — single view, no sub-navigation, mutates through states rather than existing as separate screens.
- **Bedtime log** — modal sheet (not a pushed screen) launched from Today.
- **Wake + morning check-in** — modal sheet launched from Today, energy scale + collapsed "Add detail" tag disclosure.
- **Insights** (tab 2) — three visually distinct compositions for its three states.
- **History** (tab 3) — simple log list (could-have polish deferred, base list is must-have per app_features).
- **Weekly recap** — a compact card embedded in Today (once ≥1 night logged) that opens into its own full-bleed screen, which is what ShareLink exports from. Not a fourth tab.
- **Settings** — reached via a gear icon in Today's nav bar, not a tab. Holds the non-medical disclaimer, data management, Restore Purchases.
- **Somna+ paywall** — presented as a sheet/screen from wherever a locked feature is tapped (locked Insights card, Settings upsell).

**Primary user flow:**
Launch → Today (state depends on time of day/session status) → tap "Log Tonight's Bedtime" (sheet, defaults to now, one adjustable control, one confirm; re-tapping while open edits in place) → Today shows "asleep" state (dimmed breathing card, elapsed time, "Update Bedtime" + always-visible "I'm Awake") → tap "I'm Awake" (sheet: wake time defaulted to now → tap energy scale segment → tap Done = 3 taps; tags optional behind "Add detail", doesn't count against fast path; submits fine standalone if no open bedtime session, no apologetic copy) → Today collapses to quiet "done for today" state showing last night's duration + energy + streak, explicitly reflecting the check-in, not just the bedtime tap → user browses to Insights (still-learning progress / real insight cards with one free + blurred-locked per-tag cards / no-signal card) or Recap card (share via ShareLink) → cycle repeats next bedtime.

**State model per screen:**
- **Today**: (1) first-launch empty state — moon glyph, one sentence, one CTA button; (2) pre-bedtime/evening state once ≥1 cycle exists; (3) asleep state after bedtime logged — elapsed-time card + Update Bedtime + I'm Awake; (4) done-for-today state after both logged — collapsed summary + streak, no further CTA.
- **Bedtime sheet**: create vs. edit-in-place (same sheet, keyed off whether a session is already open).
- **Wake+check-in sheet**: default (wake time = now, tags collapsed); expanded-tags; submit-as-standalone (no paired session) — all handled silently, no distinct error state.
- **Insights**: still-learning (moon-phase fill progress + encouraging copy), real-insight (one or more comparison cards; free tier sees duration-vs-energy only, per-tag cards shown with blurred bars + unlock affordance), no-signal-for-tag (visually flat/muted card, distinct from still-learning).
- **Recap**: partial-week rendering (<7 nights) vs. full-week rendering; both must render correctly, never blank.
- **Paywall**: product-loading, loaded/purchasable, purchased, restore-in-progress, load-failure (fails open, never blocks core loop).

**Visual direction:** Time-of-day-driven gradient (not tied to system light/dark mode) across fixed named bands — deep indigo-to-violet through evening/overnight, shifting to soft dawn peach/rose from early morning through midday — mapped now as 3–4 concrete bands rather than left as an aspiration, since that's the one detail most likely to silently collapse into "always dark" under build pressure. Cards: near-opaque frosted surface over the gradient, generous corner radius, soft outer glow (no hard shadows), minimal rounded typography, slow cross-fades and gentle scale only (no springs/bounce). Single soft haptic impact on the energy-scale tap, nowhere else.

**Accessibility notes:** Energy-scale control exposes itself to VoiceOver as an adjustable element with spoken value names ("Energy: Rested," not "4 of 5"); text always sits on a guaranteed-contrast card fill, never directly on raw gradient; Dynamic Type up to accessibility sizes reflows cards rather than truncating (no fixed-height insight cards); tag chips and time controls keep real 44pt targets despite compact visual size; reduced-motion collapses the moon-phase fill animation and gradient time-of-day transitions to instant cross-fades rather than removing the state information they carry.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Design Discussion

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:26:11._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Decide UX flows, information architecture, key screens/states, and the design principles that govern them.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Design Lead · User Advocate) — Round 1**

Let me lay out where I land on this, coming at it purely as the person who has to imagine someone half-asleep at 11pm and slightly groggy at 7am actually using this thing.

I'd anchor the whole IA around three tabs, not more: Today, Insights, History — with a gear icon in the Today nav bar for Settings rather than a fourth tab, since settings gets touched once and shouldn't compete for thumb real estate with the screens people actually live in. Today is the launch screen, always, no exceptions — this app should never open to a dashboard or a tab picker when the whole pitch is "two ten-second taps." Today itself needs to be a single view that mutates through a small number of states rather than four different screens: a true first-launch empty state (moon glyph, one sentence of what this app does, one button — "Log Tonight's Bedtime"), an evening/pre-bedtime state once at least one cycle exists, an "asleep" state after bedtime is logged (a dimmed, slow-breathing card showing elapsed time since bedtime, with the bedtime button now reading "Update Bedtime" and a second, equally prominent "I'm Awake" button always available — because someone might wake at 3am, 6am, or after a 20-minute nap, and the screen shouldn't assume it knows which), and a "done for today" state after both are logged, which should feel like the screen exhaling — collapsed, quiet, showing just last night's duration and energy as a small glanceable summary plus the streak, with no further call to action nagging the user. This is the single most important product decision in this whole phase: the done state has to visibly and specifically reflect the morning check-in, not just the bedtime tap, because that's the one thing standing between this app and the exact churn pattern already flagged upstream.

Bedtime logging is its own lightweight sheet, not a full screen push — time defaults to now, one adjustable control, one confirm button, and if a session's already open, that same sheet just edits it in place rather than pretending to create a new one. Wake + check-in is also a sheet, and I want to be strict about the tap count because "sub-3-taps" is easy to violate by accident: tap 1 opens the sheet (wake time already defaulted to now), tap 2 is a single tap on a 5-point energy scale rendered as large horizontal segments (not a slider, not a numeric stepper — something you can hit accurately half-asleep), tap 3 is "Done." Tags live behind a single "Add detail" disclosure that's visually present but recessed — expanding it doesn't count against the fast path, but it also can't be hidden so deep nobody ever finds it, since tags are the entire fuel for the per-tag insight engine that's supposed to justify the subscription. If there's no open bedtime session when this sheet is triggered, it still submits fine as a standalone check-in — I would not add any messaging apologizing for that or explaining it'll "count later," because that adds a reading tax to a screen that's supposed to be operable half-conscious; it should just work silently and correctly.

Insights is where I want to spend the most design care, because it's the one screen carrying the entire differentiation claim. Three states, and I'd render them as genuinely different visual compositions, not the same layout with different text swapped in: still-learning shows progress toward the threshold using the moon-phase motif itself — a crescent that visibly fills as nights accumulate, with copy like "4 more nights until your first insight," which reuses the icon system as a functional progress indicator instead of a generic percentage bar, and ties the brand mark to the thing the user is actually waiting on. Real-insight shows one or more cards, each with the associational sentence plus a small two-bar comparison (e.g., "7+ hours" vs "under 7 hours," average energy) rendered in the same soft-glow card language — free tier sees exactly one of these (duration vs. energy), and any locked per-tag insight appears as a real card with the actual comparison bars blurred and a small "Unlock with Somna+" affordance overlaid, not a generic marketing panel — showing the shape of the value you're not getting yet is a lot more honest and a lot more persuasive than hiding it entirely. No-signal-for-tag gets its own visually distinct card type — I'd use a flat/muted treatment versus the glowing insight cards, with copy like "You almost always log caffeine — we can't compare yet," so it reads as a specific observation about the user's own data, not a system error state.

The weekly recap needs to live in two places, not one: a compact card threaded into Today once there's at least one logged night (this is what gets glanced at organically), and its own full-bleed screen reachable by tapping that card, which is what ShareLink actually exports. I would not make recap a fourth tab — it's a weekly-cadence artifact, not a daily-use screen, and giving it permanent tab real estate undersells how often people would actually want it versus Today/Insights.

Visually: the gradient should be driven by real time-of-day, not just a static "sleep app is dark" theme — deep indigo-to-violet through the evening and overnight hours, shifting to soft dawn peach/rose tones roughly from early morning through midday, independent of system light/dark mode (a night-shift worker opening this at 2pm should still get a calm daytime gradient, not a jarring dark screen because their iPhone happens to be in dark mode). Cards sit on a near-opaque frosted surface over the gradient, generous corner radius, soft outer glow rather than hard shadow, minimal rounded typography, and motion that's slow cross-fades and gentle scale — no bouncy springs anywhere, because "restful" is a real constraint on the animation curve library, not just the color palette. Haptics should be a single soft impact on the energy-scale tap and nowhere else; this app should feel like the one thing on your phone that doesn't buzz at you.

Accessibility specifics I want preserved verbatim into build: the energy-scale control must expose itself as an adjustable VoiceOver element with a spoken value name ("Energy: Rested," not "4 of 5"), not just a number: cards must sit on a guaranteed-contrast fill so gradient shifts never threaten text legibility (this was already flagged upstream and the visual system needs to bake it in structurally, not rely on someone remembering it per-screen); Dynamic Type up to accessibility sizes must cause cards to reflow rather than truncate, which means no fixed-height insight cards; tag chips and the bedtime/wake time controls need real 44pt targets even though they're visually compact; and reduced-motion should collapse the moon-phase fill animation and gradient time-of-day transitions to instant cross-fades rather than skip them outright, since the state information they carry (progress, time-of-day) still needs to reach a reduced-motion user some way.

The single biggest risk to my own proposal is the time-of-day-driven gradient: it's a nice idea but it's an extra dimension of visual state that has to be explicitly mapped (which hours map to which gradient stops, what happens at the boundary) or it'll either flicker inconsistently or just get skipped under time pressure and quietly become "always dark," which would be a real loss against the spec's explicit "lightening toward dawn tones in daytime views" requirement. I'd rather see that mapping nailed down now — three or four fixed time bands, each with a named gradient — than left as a vague aspiration that the build phase interprets as "make it dark mode." The other thing I'd fight hard to keep in the final output: the "done for today" state on Today explicitly and visibly reacting to the morning check-in specifically, and the no-signal-for-tag card being visually distinct from still-learning. Both are easy to quietly merge away under build pressure, and both are the exact details this whole five-phase discussion history has been protecting.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, concrete pass that hits every required output for this phase and doesn't contradict anything locked in the six prior phases — it just turns all that accumulated detail into an actual screen-by-screen design. The big calls: three tabs only (Today/Insights/History, Settings tucked behind a gear icon rather than eating tab real estate), Today as a single mutating-state view rather than four separate screens, bedtime/wake-checkin as sheets rather than pushed screens, a real time-of-day-driven gradient system with fixed bands (not just "dark mode"), and Insights rendered as three genuinely distinct visual compositions rather than one layout with swapped text. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it explicitly protects the two details this whole discussion history has been fighting for — the "done" state visibly reacting to the morning check-in specifically, and the no-signal-for-tag card looking different from still-learning, not just reworded. Good enough to lock.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Screen inventory:**
- **Today** (tab 1, always the launch screen) — single view, no sub-navigation, mutates through states rather than existing as separate screens.
- **Bedtime log** — modal sheet (not a pushed screen) launched from Today.
- **Wake + morning check-in** — modal sheet launched from Today, energy scale + collapsed "Add detail" tag disclosure.
- **Insights** (tab 2) — three visually distinct compositions for its three states.
- **History** (tab 3) — simple log list (could-have polish deferred, base list is must-have per app_features).
- **Weekly recap** — a compact card embedded in Today (once ≥1 night logged) that opens into its own full-bleed screen, which is what ShareLink exports from. Not a fourth tab.
- **Settings** — reached via a gear icon in Today's nav bar, not a tab. Holds the non-medical disclaimer, data management, Restore Purchases.
- **Somna+ paywall** — presented as a sheet/screen from wherever a locked feature is tapped (locked Insights card, Settings upsell).

**Primary user flow:**
Launch → Today (state depends on time of day/session status) → tap "Log Tonight's Bedtime" (sheet, defaults to now, one adjustable control, one confirm; re-tapping while open edits in place) → Today shows "asleep" state (dimmed breathing card, elapsed time, "Update Bedtime" + always-visible "I'm Awake") → tap "I'm Awake" (sheet: wake time defaulted to now → tap energy scale segment → tap Done = 3 taps; tags optional behind "Add detail", doesn't count against fast path; submits fine standalone if no open bedtime session, no apologetic copy) → Today collapses to quiet "done for today" state showing last night's duration + energy + streak, explicitly reflecting the check-in, not just the bedtime tap → user browses to Insights (still-learning progress / real insight cards with one free + blurred-locked per-tag cards / no-signal card) or Recap card (share via ShareLink) → cycle repeats next bedtime.

**State model per screen:**
- **Today**: (1) first-launch empty state — moon glyph, one sentence, one CTA button; (2) pre-bedtime/evening state once ≥1 cycle exists; (3) asleep state after bedtime logged — elapsed-time card + Update Bedtime + I'm Awake; (4) done-for-today state after both logged — collapsed summary + streak, no further CTA.
- **Bedtime sheet**: create vs. edit-in-place (same sheet, keyed off whether a session is already open).
- **Wake+check-in sheet**: default (wake time = now, tags collapsed); expanded-tags; submit-as-standalone (no paired session) — all handled silently, no distinct error state.
- **Insights**: still-learning (moon-phase fill progress + encouraging copy), real-insight (one or more comparison cards; free tier sees duration-vs-energy only, per-tag cards shown with blurred bars + unlock affordance), no-signal-for-tag (visually flat/muted card, distinct from still-learning).
- **Recap**: partial-week rendering (<7 nights) vs. full-week rendering; both must render correctly, never blank.
- **Paywall**: product-loading, loaded/purchasable, purchased, restore-in-progress, load-failure (fails open, never blocks core loop).

**Visual direction:** Time-of-day-driven gradient (not tied to system light/dark mode) across fixed named bands — deep indigo-to-violet through evening/overnight, shifting to soft dawn peach/rose from early morning through midday — mapped now as 3–4 concrete bands rather than left as an aspiration, since that's the one detail most likely to silently collapse into "always dark" under build pressure. Cards: near-opaque frosted surface over the gradient, generous corner radius, soft outer glow (no hard shadows), minimal rounded typography, slow cross-fades and gentle scale only (no springs/bounce). Single soft haptic impact on the energy-scale tap, nowhere else.

**Accessibility notes:** Energy-scale control exposes itself to VoiceOver as an adjustable element with spoken value names ("Energy: Rested," not "4 of 5"); text always sits on a guaranteed-contrast card fill, never directly on raw gradient; Dynamic Type up to accessibility sizes reflows cards rather than truncating (no fixed-height insight cards); tag chips and time controls keep real 44pt targets despite compact visual size; reduced-motion collapses the moon-phase fill animation and gradient time-of-day transitions to instant cross-fades rather than removing the state information they carry.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. This design pass is concrete and buildable, covers all five required outputs, and doesn't contradict any of the six locked prior phases — it operationalizes them into a real screen/state model.

- **Screen inventory**: Complete set (Today, Bedtime sheet, Wake+check-in sheet, Insights, History, Recap, Settings, Paywall), correctly resolves the tab-count question (3 tabs, Settings behind a gear icon, Recap embedded not tabbed) with sound reasoning tied to usage frequency.
- **Primary user flow**: Traces the full core loop end-to-end (launch → bedtime → asleep state → wake+check-in → done state → Insights/Recap → repeat), matching the locked core loop from `initial_discussion`/`per_app_product_brief` and respecting the ≤3-tap fast path and standalone check-in rule from `detailed_discussion`/`app_features`.
- **State model per screen**: Every screen has an explicit state enumeration, including the two details this discussion history has repeatedly flagged as at-risk of being merged away — the done-state reacting specifically to the check-in, and no-signal-for-tag being visually distinct from still-learning. Paywall states correctly include fail-open per the locked StoreKit requirement.
- **Visual direction**: Resolves the design-direction ambiguity from the spec with a concrete, buildable answer (named time-of-day bands, not "dark mode"), correctly flagged by the coordinator as the biggest execution risk worth pinning down now rather than leaving vague.
- **Accessibility notes**: Specific and testable (VoiceOver adjustable trait with spoken value names, guaranteed-contrast card fills, Dynamic Type reflow, 44pt targets, reduced-motion fallback that preserves state information rather than just disabling animation) — matches the accessibility requirements locked in `detailed_discussion`.

Acceptance checks satisfied: a SwiftUI developer can build the view hierarchy directly from this (sheets vs. tabs vs. embedded cards are all explicit), and every must-have workflow from `app_features` has a screen/state path. Ready to close.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, concrete pass that hits every required output for this phase and doesn't contradict anything locked in the six prior phases — it just turns all that accumulated detail into an actual screen-by-screen design. The big calls: three tabs only (Today/Insights/History, Settings tucked behind a gear icon rather than eating tab real estate), Today as a single mutating-state view rather than four separate screens, bedtime/wake-checkin as sheets rather than pushed screens, a real time-of-day-driven gradient system with fixed bands (not just "dark mode"), and Insights rendered as three genuinely distinct visual compositions rather than one layout with swapped text. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it explicitly protects the two details this whole discussion history has been fighting for — the "done" state visibly reacting to the morning check-in specifically, and the no-signal-for-tag card looking different from still-learning, not just reworded. Good enough to lock.

CONSENSUS: YES
