phase_discussions.title: Design Handoff
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Only one voice showed up again this round (Codex's still in cooldown), but the pass is genuinely complete and hits every output this phase requires — it's not just restating the design_discussion screen list, it's the builder-ready translation of it: real SwiftUI types, concrete token values, a named component library, and specific engineering guardrails (TimelineView over Timer, async off-main-actor correlation recompute, a single shared clock feeding both the elapsed-time label and the gradient so they don't drift). Nothing here contradicts anything locked in the six prior phases — it operationalizes the "done state must react to the check-in specifically" and "no-signal card must be structurally distinct" concerns that design_discussion fought for, by naming the exact mechanism (a `hasAnimatedDoneStateToday` flag, a real `NoSignalCard` view rather than a reskinned `StillLearningCard`) that keeps those decisions from eroding during implementation. It also directly answers the "external design or not" question the phase playbook requires, with a reasoned call (build the system in-code since it's already concrete enough; only scope a narrow external Claude Design prompt for the app icon specifically) rather than dodging it. Ready to lock.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen design specification:**

- **Today** — single `TodayView`, one hero card that cross-fades its internal content across four states (empty / pre-bedtime / asleep / done-for-today) rather than four separate views, plus a compact recap card once ≥1 night exists. Driven by one root `TimelineView(.everyMinute)` whose `context.date` is passed down to both the elapsed-time label and the gradient — no independent clocks per subview. Empty: moon glyph, one sentence, one full-width primary CTA. Pre-bedtime: same CTA + streak/last-night strip. Asleep: ticking elapsed time, de-emphasized "Update Bedtime," always-tappable "I'm Awake" (never disabled by elapsed duration, to support 14+ hour shift-worker sessions). Done-for-today: CTA disappears, shows last night's duration + energy-in-words + streak, with a one-time-per-day checkmark micro-motion gated by a `hasAnimatedDoneStateToday` flag so it doesn't replay on every foreground.
- **Bedtime sheet** — `.sheet` with `.presentationDetents([.medium])`, one `DatePicker` defaulting to now + one confirm button; same view edits in place (title/label swap to "Update Bedtime") when a session's already open, driven by a boolean not a second sheet.
- **Wake + check-in sheet** — same medium-detent sheet: wake-time picker (defaults to now) → 5 equal-width tappable energy segments (not a slider) which immediately enable Done and fire the app's one haptic → collapsed "Add detail" disclosure expanding into a `LazyVGrid` of tag chips → Done. Fully optional tag state; submits identically whether collapsed, expanded, or standalone with no open session — no apologetic copy anywhere.
- **Insights** — `ScrollView` of cards, deliberately not a `List` (unwanted separators/swipe actions fight the custom card layouts). Still-learning: hero card with a custom animatable-`Shape` moon-crescent-fill (not a system `ProgressView`) + "N more nights" copy. Real-insight: title sentence + hand-rolled two-`Capsule` comparison bars; the same `InsightCard` component takes an `isLocked` modifier that blurs the bars and overlays an unlock affordance, so the locked card is never a separately-built stub. No-signal: a structurally distinct `NoSignalCard` (flatter fill, no glow) — explicitly not a reskin of the still-learning card.
- **History** — plain `List`, date/duration/energy-icon rows, delete-only swipe action (no edit here — editing stays confined to Today's asleep-state "Update Bedtime" per the locked edit-scope rule), with its own empty state for first launch.
- **Recap** — compact fixed-height card embedded in Today, tapping it opens a full-bleed `RecapDetailView` that is the literal `ShareLink` source via `ImageRenderer`, so shared output can never visually drift from the live screen.
- **Settings** — grouped `Form`: disclaimer, data management (delete-all behind a confirmation alert), Restore Purchases, paywall entry point.
- **Paywall** — sheet over `SubscriptionStoreView`/custom screen covering loading/purchasable/purchased/restoring/load-failed; load-failed renders inline retry + dismiss, never a blocking spinner, and dismissing never leaves any part of Today gated.

**Design tokens:**
- Four time-of-day gradient bands — `duskDeep` (21:00–00:00, indigo→violet), `overnight` (00:00–05:00, deep violet-black), `dawn` (05:00–10:00, violet→peach/rose), `day` (10:00–21:00, soft warm neutral) — each a `LinearGradient` with 2–3 stops, linearly cross-faded over a ~30-minute window at boundaries via a pure, unit-testable `gradient(for: Date) -> [Color]` function.
- Corner radius: 24pt (hero/insight cards), 16pt (chips/small components).
- Card fill: fixed near-opaque `Color.cardSurface` (~85–90% opacity) — explicitly not `.ultraThinMaterial`, since translucent system materials would let the gradient bleed through inconsistently and threaten guaranteed contrast.
- Glow: single shared `.glowCard()` modifier (soft shadow, radius 20, low opacity) — never hand-rolled per screen.
- Type: rounded system font, five sizes (title/headline/body/caption/numeral-large), full Dynamic Type support, no hard caps.
- Motion: one `.easeInOut(duration: 0.4)` cross-fade curve everywhere, one gentle spring `(response: 0.5, dampingFraction: 0.85)` reserved only for the moon-crescent-fill and the done-state checkmark.
- Haptic: exactly one, `.impact(.soft)`, fired only on energy-scale selection.

**Component inventory:** `GradientBackground`, `FrostedCard`, `MoonCrescentProgress`, `EnergyScaleControl` (a11y-critical — ships with `.accessibilityAdjustableAction`/`.accessibilityValue` from day one, not retrofitted), `TagChip`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton` (44pt minimum baked into the style), `InsightCard` (real/locked/no-signal via a config enum, not three separate views), `StreakBadge`, `RecapCardContent` (shared between embedded and full-bleed so ShareLink output can't drift).

**State and interaction notes:** Correlation recompute runs async off the main actor on each foreground, publishing into view state while the view continues showing previous values until new ones land — satisfies "always fresh, never cached" without risking a visible hitch as history grows under unlimited-history paying users. SwiftData "open session" queries use a real predicate (`endDate == nil`), not a hand-reconstructed check in the view model, so edit-in-place and auto-pairing logic can't disagree about what's open under concurrent taps.

**Accessibility requirements (carried forward + sharpened):** `EnergyScaleControl` exposes VoiceOver value/adjustable-trait support as a first-class build requirement, not an add-on; all text sits on the opaque `cardSurface` fill, never the raw gradient; Dynamic Type reflows cards (no fixed-height insight cards); 44pt targets enforced structurally via the button/chip components rather than left to call sites; reduced-motion collapses the crescent-fill and gradient transitions to instant cross-fades without dropping the state information they carry.

**Claude Design prompt / external-design decision:** No pause for external design on the main app — the token values, gradient bands, and component set are already concrete enough that building directly in SwiftUI avoids a translation/drift risk between a separate design tool's output and real `LinearGradient`/shadow values. The one piece worth an external pass is the app icon specifically: *"Design an app icon for Somna, a calm sleep-tracking app: a minimalist moon-phase mark (crescent transitioning through phases), set against a deep indigo-to-violet gradient consistent with dusk-to-dawn, no text, no clinical iconography, soft-glow edge treatment, must read clearly at 60×60pt."*

# somna — Design Handoff

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:28:36._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.
```

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Systems Thinker) — Round 1**

Picking up where design_discussion left off, my job this round is to turn that screen/state model into something a SwiftUI engineer can actually type in without guessing, and to flag the places where "looks fine in a mockup" quietly turns into "janky or wrong on a real device." I'll go screen by screen, then tokens/components, then the systems-level risks I think the handoff needs to nail down explicitly.

Today is a single `TodayView` backed by a lightweight view model that derives its state from three inputs: the most recent open-or-closed sleep session, today's check-in (if any), and current wall-clock time via `TimelineView(.everyMinute)` rather than a retained `Timer` — a `Timer` that keeps firing while the view is off-screen is a real battery/correctness footgun for a "breathing card" that's supposed to look calm, and `TimelineView` gets you the elapsed-time re-render and the gradient re-render on the same clock for free without you managing invalidation yourself. Layout is a `ScrollView` with a single hero card at top (whichever state is active) and, once ≥1 night exists, the compact recap card below it — the hero card owns all four states (first-launch empty, pre-bedtime, asleep, done-for-today) as one view that cross-fades its internal content rather than four separate views being swapped, so the container chrome (card shape, glow, position) never jumps. The empty state is: moon glyph, one sentence, one full-width primary button ("Log Tonight's Bedtime"). Pre-bedtime state (≥1 cycle exists, no session open) is the same button plus a small streak/last-night summary strip above it. Asleep state shows elapsed time ticking, a de-emphasized "Update Bedtime" text button, and a full-width primary "I'm Awake" button that's always tappable regardless of how much time has passed — no disabling logic based on elapsed time, because a night-shift worker's session can legitimately run 14+ hours. Done-for-today state drops the CTA entirely and shows last night's duration, the energy value in words (not a number), and the streak count, with a subtle checkmark-in-moon micro-motion the first time it appears that session (not on every re-render — this needs a "hasAnimatedDoneStateToday" flag in the view model, otherwise it replays every time the user backgrounds/foregrounds the app, which reads as broken rather than delightful).

Bedtime and wake+check-in are both `.sheet` presentations with `.presentationDetents([.medium])` rather than full-screen — medium height keeps Today visible-ish behind them and reinforces that these are quick interruptions, not destinations. The bedtime sheet is one `DatePicker` (compact/wheel style, defaulting to `Date.now`) and one confirm button; if a session is already open, the sheet's title and confirm-button label switch to "Update Bedtime" and it edits in place — same view, driven by a boolean, not two separate sheets. The wake+check-in sheet is the one place I'd slow down and be precise about layout: wake-time picker at top (defaults to now), then the energy control as five large equal-width horizontal segments below it (not a slider — sliders are bad for half-asleep thumbs and bad for VoiceOver unless you build a lot of extra support), then a single-line "Add detail" disclosure row that expands downward into a `LazyVGrid` of tag chips (caffeine, exercise, screen time, stress, alcohol — whatever the fixed set ends up being), then "Done." Selecting an energy segment should immediately enable Done (don't require a second confirmation tap on the segment itself) and fire the one haptic impact this app is allowed to have. Submitting with the sheet mid-expanded-tags or fully collapsed both need to work identically — the tag section is genuinely optional state, not a required step the sheet is silently tracking as incomplete.

Insights is a `ScrollView` of cards, not a `List` — `List` gives you separators and swipe actions you don't want here, and the still-learning and no-signal cards need custom internal layout that fights `List` row styling. Still-learning renders one hero card with the crescent-fill moon motif (a custom `Shape` with an animatable fill fraction, not a `ProgressView` — you want the moon-phase motif to double as the progress indicator, which means it can't be a generic system component) plus "N more nights until your first insight." Real-insight cards render a title sentence, then a small two-bar horizontal comparison (Swift Charts `BarMark` is fine here, or a hand-rolled two-`Capsule` view if Charts feels heavier than needed for two bars — I'd lean hand-rolled since Charts' default styling will fight the frosted/glow card language). The locked per-tag card is the same card type with `.blur(radius:)` over the bar comparison and an unlock affordance on top — meaning the insight-card component needs a `isLocked: Bool` modifier, not a separate locked-card view, so the real data shape is always what's rendered underneath. The no-signal card is a distinct, flatter component (`NoSignalCard`, no glow modifier, muted fill) — this is the one detail I'd flag as easiest to accidentally implement as "StillLearningCard with different text," and it needs to stay a structurally different SwiftUI view for that distinction to survive future edits.

History is a plain `List` of session rows (date, duration, energy icon), each row's swipe actions exposing delete only — edit is not available from History per the locked rule that editing is limited to the most-recent/open session, so the edit affordance lives only on Today's asleep-state "Update Bedtime," not here. This needs an explicit empty state (moon glyph, "Nothing logged yet") since History is trivially reachable with zero data on first launch.

Recap: the compact card embedded in Today is a fixed-height summary (avg sleep, best night, check-in completion this week) that navigates to a full-bleed `RecapDetailView` on tap; that detail view is what's wrapped in `ShareLink`, rendered via `ImageRenderer` to produce the shareable card image so what's shared matches what's on screen exactly rather than a separately-coded export layout drifting from the live view over time.

Settings is a plain grouped `Form` — disclaimer text, data management (delete all data, with a confirmation alert since that's destructive locally), Restore Purchases, and a link to the Somna+ paywall if not subscribed. Paywall is a sheet using `SubscriptionStoreView` or a custom screen over it per the loaded/purchasable/purchased/restoring/load-failed states already locked — load-failed should render the same screen with an inline "couldn't load prices, try again" and a dismiss button, never a blocking spinner, and dismissing it must never leave any part of Today gated.

On tokens: four fixed gradient bands as agreed — call them `duskDeep` (roughly 21:00–00:00, indigo to violet), `overnight` (00:00–05:00, deepest violet-black), `dawn` (05:00–10:00, violet fading to peach/rose), `day` (10:00–21:00, soft warm neutral, still calm not clinical-white). Each band is a `LinearGradient` token with two-to-three stops; interpolate linearly between adjacent bands' stops over a ~30 minute crossfade window at each boundary rather than hard-cutting, computed as a function of `Date.now` each time `TimelineView` ticks — this is cheap (a couple of color lerps) so there's no real perf concern, but it does mean the gradient function needs to be pure and testable (`gradient(for: Date) -> [Color]`) so it can get a unit test asserting band boundaries without spinning up a view. Corner radius token: 24pt for hero/insight cards, 16pt for chips/small components. Card fill: a fixed near-opaque material (`Color.cardSurface` at ~85–90% opacity, not `.ultraThinMaterial`, since translucent system materials will let the gradient bleed through inconsistently across light/dark system settings and threaten the guaranteed-contrast requirement). Glow token: a soft `.shadow(color: accentGlow, radius: 20, y: 8)` at low opacity, applied once via a shared `.glowCard()` view modifier so it's never hand-rolled per screen. Type scale: rounded system font (`.rounded` design), five sizes (title, headline, body, caption, numeral-large for durations), all using `.dynamicTypeSize` and none hard-capped. Motion tokens: one easing curve, `.easeInOut(duration: 0.4)` for cross-fades, one gentle scale spring `(response: 0.5, dampingFraction: 0.85)` reserved for the single moon-crescent-fill and done-state checkmark moments — nowhere else. Haptic token: exactly one, `.impact(.soft)`, fired only from the energy-scale selection.

Component inventory to hand to the builder as a shared library before screens get built: `GradientBackground` (time-driven, takes no per-screen config), `FrostedCard` (the base card shape + fill + glow modifier), `MoonCrescentProgress` (custom Shape, animatable fill), `EnergyScaleControl` (the accessibility-critical one — needs `.accessibilityAdjustableAction` and `.accessibilityValue` wired from day one, not retrofitted), `TagChip`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton` (44pt min height baked into the style, not left to call sites), `InsightCard` (real/locked/no-signal via enum, not three views), `StreakBadge`, `RecapCardContent` (shared between embedded and full-bleed so ShareLink output matches).

The systems-level risk I'd push hardest on: correlation recompute is specified as "always fresh on foreground, never cached." That's correct for correctness, but if it's naively called on the main actor inside `.onAppear`/`scenePhase` handling, and the correlation engine ever runs over a couple hundred nights with several tag types, that's a real risk of a visible hitch right when Insights appears — small today, but this app's whole pitch is "unlimited history" for paying users, so the computation isn't guaranteed to stay cheap as retention grows. I'd have the engine run as an `async` function off the main actor, published into the view via `@Published`/`@Observable` state, with the view showing its previous values until the new ones land rather than blocking — that's not a "cached and stale" violation, it's just not letting an async recompute freeze a scroll gesture. Second risk: the "asleep" elapsed-time card and the gradient both want to tick on a clock, and if they're driven by two separate `TimelineView`s with different schedules you'll get micro-jitter between the elapsed-time label and the background updating; I'd have one `TimelineView(.everyMinute)` at the `TodayView` root and pass `context.date` down, not nest independent clocks per subview. Third: SwiftData's `@Query` sort/filter for "is there an open session" needs to be a real predicate (`endDate == nil`), not something reconstructed by hand in the view model on every render, or the edit-in-place bedtime logic and the auto-pairing logic can disagree about what counts as "open" under concurrent taps.

On the external-design question: I don't think this needs a pause for a separate Claude Design pass — the visual system here is concrete enough (named gradient bands, explicit token values, a defined component set) that generating it inside the SwiftUI build directly is faster and keeps the token values and the implementation from drifting apart, which is a real risk if a separate design tool produces static mockups that then have to be manually re-translated into `LinearGradient` stops and shadow values. If the coordinator still wants an external pass for icon/App Store asset exploration specifically — the moon-phase app icon mark — I'd scope a Claude Design prompt narrowly to just that: "Design an app icon for Somna, a calm sleep-tracking app: a minimalist moon-phase mark (crescent transitioning through phases), set against a deep indigo-to-violet gradient consistent with dusk-to-dawn, no text, no clinical iconography, soft-glow edge treatment, must read clearly at 60x60pt." Everything else — screens, cards, controls — I'd build straight from this handoff rather than round-tripping through an external tool.

## Final Output

**Screen-by-screen design specification:**

- **Today** — single `TodayView`, one hero card that cross-fades its internal content across four states (empty / pre-bedtime / asleep / done-for-today) rather than four separate views, plus a compact recap card once ≥1 night exists. Driven by one root `TimelineView(.everyMinute)` whose `context.date` is passed down to both the elapsed-time label and the gradient — no independent clocks per subview. Empty: moon glyph, one sentence, one full-width primary CTA. Pre-bedtime: same CTA + streak/last-night strip. Asleep: ticking elapsed time, de-emphasized "Update Bedtime," always-tappable "I'm Awake" (never disabled by elapsed duration, to support 14+ hour shift-worker sessions). Done-for-today: CTA disappears, shows last night's duration + energy-in-words + streak, with a one-time-per-day checkmark micro-motion gated by a `hasAnimatedDoneStateToday` flag so it doesn't replay on every foreground.
- **Bedtime sheet** — `.sheet` with `.presentationDetents([.medium])`, one `DatePicker` defaulting to now + one confirm button; same view edits in place (title/label swap to "Update Bedtime") when a session's already open, driven by a boolean not a second sheet.
- **Wake + check-in sheet** — same medium-detent sheet: wake-time picker (defaults to now) → 5 equal-width tappable energy segments (not a slider) which immediately enable Done and fire the app's one haptic → collapsed "Add detail" disclosure expanding into a `LazyVGrid` of tag chips → Done. Fully optional tag state; submits identically whether collapsed, expanded, or standalone with no open session — no apologetic copy anywhere.
- **Insights** — `ScrollView` of cards, deliberately not a `List` (unwanted separators/swipe actions fight the custom card layouts). Still-learning: hero card with a custom animatable-`Shape` moon-crescent-fill (not a system `ProgressView`) + "N more nights" copy. Real-insight: title sentence + hand-rolled two-`Capsule` comparison bars; the same `InsightCard` component takes an `isLocked` modifier that blurs the bars and overlays an unlock affordance, so the locked card is never a separately-built stub. No-signal: a structurally distinct `NoSignalCard` (flatter fill, no glow) — explicitly not a reskin of the still-learning card.
- **History** — plain `List`, date/duration/energy-icon rows, delete-only swipe action (no edit here — editing stays confined to Today's asleep-state "Update Bedtime" per the locked edit-scope rule), with its own empty state for first launch.
- **Recap** — compact fixed-height card embedded in Today, tapping it opens a full-bleed `RecapDetailView` that is the literal `ShareLink` source via `ImageRenderer`, so shared output can never visually drift from the live screen.
- **Settings** — grouped `Form`: disclaimer, data management (delete-all behind a confirmation alert), Restore Purchases, paywall entry point.
- **Paywall** — sheet over `SubscriptionStoreView`/custom screen covering loading/purchasable/purchased/restoring/load-failed; load-failed renders inline retry + dismiss, never a blocking spinner, and dismissing never leaves any part of Today gated.

**Design tokens:**
- Four time-of-day gradient bands — `duskDeep` (21:00–00:00, indigo→violet), `overnight` (00:00–05:00, deep violet-black), `dawn` (05:00–10:00, violet→peach/rose), `day` (10:00–21:00, soft warm neutral) — each a `LinearGradient` with 2–3 stops, linearly cross-faded over a ~30-minute window at boundaries via a pure, unit-testable `gradient(for: Date) -> [Color]` function.
- Corner radius: 24pt (hero/insight cards), 16pt (chips/small components).
- Card fill: fixed near-opaque `Color.cardSurface` (~85–90% opacity) — explicitly not `.ultraThinMaterial`, since translucent system materials would let the gradient bleed through inconsistently and threaten guaranteed contrast.
- Glow: single shared `.glowCard()` modifier (soft shadow, radius 20, low opacity) — never hand-rolled per screen.
- Type: rounded system font, five sizes (title/headline/body/caption/numeral-large), full Dynamic Type support, no hard caps.
- Motion: one `.easeInOut(duration: 0.4)` cross-fade curve everywhere, one gentle spring `(response: 0.5, dampingFraction: 0.85)` reserved only for the moon-crescent-fill and the done-state checkmark.
- Haptic: exactly one, `.impact(.soft)`, fired only on energy-scale selection.

**Component inventory:** `GradientBackground`, `FrostedCard`, `MoonCrescentProgress`, `EnergyScaleControl` (a11y-critical — ships with `.accessibilityAdjustableAction`/`.accessibilityValue` from day one, not retrofitted), `TagChip`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton` (44pt minimum baked into the style), `InsightCard` (real/locked/no-signal via a config enum, not three separate views), `StreakBadge`, `RecapCardContent` (shared between embedded and full-bleed so ShareLink output can't drift).

**State and interaction notes:** Correlation recompute runs async off the main actor on each foreground, publishing into view state while the view continues showing previous values until new ones land — satisfies "always fresh, never cached" without risking a visible hitch as history grows under unlimited-history paying users. SwiftData "open session" queries use a real predicate (`endDate == nil`), not a hand-reconstructed check in the view model, so edit-in-place and auto-pairing logic can't disagree about what's open under concurrent taps.

**Accessibility requirements (carried forward + sharpened):** `EnergyScaleControl` exposes VoiceOver value/adjustable-trait support as a first-class build requirement, not an add-on; all text sits on the opaque `cardSurface` fill, never the raw gradient; Dynamic Type reflows cards (no fixed-height insight cards); 44pt targets enforced structurally via the button/chip components rather than left to call sites; reduced-motion collapses the crescent-fill and gradient transitions to instant cross-fades without dropping the state information they carry.

**Claude Design prompt / external-design decision:** No pause for external design on the main app — the token values, gradient bands, and component set are already concrete enough that building directly in SwiftUI avoids a translation/drift risk between a separate design tool's output and real `LinearGradient`/shadow values. The one piece worth an external pass is the app icon specifically: *"Design an app icon for Somna, a calm sleep-tracking app: a minimalist moon-phase mark (crescent transitioning through phases), set against a deep indigo-to-violet gradient consistent with dusk-to-dawn, no text, no clinical iconography, soft-glow edge treatment, must read clearly at 60×60pt."*

## Feedback
Passes. All six required outputs are present, concrete, and internally consistent with the six prior locked phases:

- **Screen-by-screen spec**: Every primary screen (Today, Bedtime sheet, Wake+check-in sheet, Insights, History, Recap, Settings, Paywall) has real SwiftUI types, explicit state variants, and layout intent — not adjectives. Motion guidance is present where it matters (Today's cross-fade between states, the gated one-time checkmark, Insights' animatable moon-crescent shape) rather than left as a generic "add animations" note.
- **Design tokens**: Concrete, implementable values — four named gradient bands with a pure/testable `gradient(for: Date)` function, explicit corner radii, a named opaque card-fill token (correctly rejecting `.ultraThinMaterial` for a contrast reason, not a stylistic one), a single shared glow modifier, a bounded type scale, and exactly two motion curves with defined scope. This is what "premium and category-specific" looks like in token form rather than left to per-screen improvisation.
- **Component inventory**: Named, reusable, and correctly scoped — notably `InsightCard` as one component with a locked/no-signal config rather than three divergent views, which directly protects the no-signal-vs-still-learning distinction design_discussion fought for.
- **State and interaction notes**: Resolves two real engineering risks the prior phase's design left implicit — async off-main-actor correlation recompute (protects "always fresh" from becoming a scroll-hitch as history grows for paying Somna+ users), and a real SwiftData predicate for "open session" (prevents edit-in-place and auto-pairing logic from disagreeing under concurrent taps).
- **Accessibility**: Specific and testable, not boilerplate — VoiceOver adjustable-trait wiring named as day-one for `EnergyScaleControl`, contrast guaranteed structurally via the card-fill token, Dynamic Type reflow, structural 44pt enforcement, and reduced-motion that preserves state information rather than just disabling animation.
- **External-design decision**: Explicit and reasoned (build the system in-code to avoid token/implementation drift), with a properly scoped, ready-to-use Claude Design prompt for the one piece that's genuinely a visual-exploration task (the app icon) rather than a blanket punt.

Acceptance checks satisfied: a SwiftUI builder can implement directly from this (real type names, concrete values, no "TBD" gaps), nothing is a hidden decision, and the direction is category-specific (time-of-day-driven gradient bands, moon-phase motif reused functionally as a progress indicator) rather than generic dark-mode styling. No contradiction with any prior phase — it operationalizes design_discussion's two hardest-won details (done-state reacting to the check-in specifically, no-signal card structurally distinct) with concrete mechanisms. Ready to close.

- **Bedtime sheet** — `.sheet` with `.presentationDetents([.medium])`, one `DatePicker` defaulting to now + one confirm button; same view edits in place (title/label swap to "Update Bedtime") when a session's already open, driven by a boolean not a second sheet.
- **Wake + check-in sheet** — same medium-detent sheet: wake-time picker (defaults to now) → 5 equal-width tappable energy segments (not a slider) which immediately enable Done and fire the app's one haptic → collapsed "Add detail" disclosure expanding into a `LazyVGrid` of tag chips → Done. Fully optional tag state; submits identically whether collapsed, expanded, or standalone with no open session — no apologetic copy anywhere.
- **Insights** — `ScrollView` of cards, deliberately not a `List` (unwanted separators/swipe actions fight the custom card layouts). Still-learning: hero card with a custom animatable-`Shape` moon-crescent-fill (not a system `ProgressView`) + "N more nights" copy. Real-insight: title sentence + hand-rolled two-`Capsule` comparison bars; the same `InsightCard` component takes an `isLocked` modifier that blurs the bars and overlays an unlock affordance, so the locked card is never a separately-built stub. No-signal: a structurally distinct `NoSignalCard` (flatter fill, no glow) — explicitly not a reskin of the still-learning card.
- **History** — plain `List`, date/duration/energy-icon rows, delete-only swipe action (no edit here — editing stays confined to Today's asleep-state "Update Bedtime" per the locked edit-scope rule), with its own empty state for first launch.
- **Recap** — compact fixed-height card embedded in Today, tapping it opens a full-bleed `RecapDetailView` that is the literal `ShareLink` source via `ImageRenderer`, so shared output can never visually drift from the live screen.
- **Settings** — grouped `Form`: disclaimer, data management (delete-all behind a confirmation alert), Restore Purchases, paywall entry point.
- **Paywall** — sheet over `SubscriptionStoreView`/custom screen covering loading/purchasable/purchased/restoring/load-failed; load-failed renders inline retry + dismiss, never a blocking spinner, and dismissing never leaves any part of Today gated.

**Design tokens:**
- Four time-of-day gradient bands — `duskDeep` (21:00–00:00, indigo→violet), `overnight` (00:00–05:00, deep violet-black), `dawn` (05:00–10:00, violet→peach/rose), `day` (10:00–21:00, soft warm neutral) — each a `LinearGradient` with 2–3 stops, linearly cross-faded over a ~30-minute window at boundaries via a pure, unit-testable `gradient(for: Date) -> [Color]` function.
- Corner radius: 24pt (hero/insight cards), 16pt (chips/small components).
- Card fill: fixed near-opaque `Color.cardSurface` (~85–90% opacity) — explicitly not `.ultraThinMaterial`, since translucent system materials would let the gradient bleed through inconsistently and threaten guaranteed contrast.
- Glow: single shared `.glowCard()` modifier (soft shadow, radius 20, low opacity) — never hand-rolled per screen.
- Type: rounded system font, five sizes (title/headline/body/caption/numeral-large), full Dynamic Type support, no hard caps.
- Motion: one `.easeInOut(duration: 0.4)` cross-fade curve everywhere, one gentle spring `(response: 0.5, dampingFraction: 0.85)` reserved only for the moon-crescent-fill and the done-state checkmark.
- Haptic: exactly one, `.impact(.soft)`, fired only on energy-scale selection.

**Component inventory:** `GradientBackground`, `FrostedCard`, `MoonCrescentProgress`, `EnergyScaleControl` (a11y-critical — ships with `.accessibilityAdjustableAction`/`.accessibilityValue` from day one, not retrofitted), `TagChip`, `PrimaryButton`/`SecondaryButton` (44pt minimum baked into the style), `InsightCard` (real/locked/no-signal via a config enum, not three separate views), `StreakBadge`, `RecapCardContent` (shared between embedded and full-bleed so ShareLink output can't drift).

**State and interaction notes:** Correlation recompute runs async off the main actor on each foreground, publishing into view state while the view continues showing previous values until new ones land — satisfies "always fresh, never cached" without risking a visible hitch as history grows under unlimited-history paying users. SwiftData "open session" queries use a real predicate (`endDate == nil`), not a hand-reconstructed check in the view model, so edit-in-place and auto-pairing logic can't disagree about what's open under concurrent taps.

**Accessibility requirements (carried forward + sharpened):** `EnergyScaleControl` exposes VoiceOver value/adjustable-trait support as a first-class build requirement, not an add-on; all text sits on the opaque `cardSurface` fill, never the raw gradient; Dynamic Type reflows cards (no fixed-height insight cards); 44pt targets enforced structurally via the button/chip components rather than left to call sites; reduced-motion collapses the crescent-fill and gradient transitions to instant cross-fades without dropping the state information they carry.

**Claude Design prompt / external-design decision:** No pause for external design on the main app — the token values, gradient bands, and component set are already concrete enough that building directly in SwiftUI avoids a translation/drift risk between a separate design tool's output and real `LinearGradient`/shadow values. The one piece worth an external pass is the app icon specifically: *"Design an app icon for Somna, a calm sleep-tracking app: a minimalist moon-phase mark (crescent transitioning through phases), set against a deep indigo-to-violet gradient consistent with dusk-to-dawn, no text, no clinical iconography, soft-glow edge treatment, must read clearly at 60×60pt."*

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Ios Architecture Review
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, decisive pass that closes out every architecture question this phase needs answered, and it doesn't fight anything locked in the seven prior phases — it just makes concrete, buildable calls on things design_handoff left as engineering-level detail. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it's specific enough to hand straight to tech_specs.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** `@Observable` view models (iOS 17+, not `ObservableObject`/Combine). Two repository protocols — `SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository` — injected via `@Environment`, so SwiftData can be swapped for an in-memory fake in tests and later for CloudKit without touching views. The correlation engine is a real Swift `actor` (not just "an async function") so the off-main-actor guarantee design_handoff already committed to is actually data-race-safe, not accidentally safe today. Critically, the engine's input type is plain structs (arrays of session/check-in value types), never `@Model` objects directly — that's what keeps the threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) unit-testable without a `ModelContainer`, and prevents someone from "simplifying" it later by querying SwiftData inside the engine.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftData (two `@Model` types, `SleepSession` with `endDate: Date?` for a real `endDate == nil` predicate, and `CheckIn`); StoreKit 2 (`.storekit` config for local testing, `Transaction.updates` listened from launch, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` as source of truth — never a cached bool); ActivityKit for the Live Activity; WidgetKit for the widget; HealthKit read-only, opt-in only from Settings, never requested at first launch; explicitly no UserNotifications framework anywhere, consistent with the locked no-push decision; no Charts — hand-rolled `Capsule` bars instead, matching the frosted-card aesthetic and cutting a framework surface to test.

**Persistence plan:** A versioned SwiftData schema (`VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan`) from the first commit, even with only one version today — non-negotiable given "unlimited history" is the paid value prop, so real users will accumulate months of data before any future schema change, and retrofitting migration onto an unversioned store later is far worse than declaring one now.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** HealthKit denied-vs-not-determined is indistinguishable by design — never inferred or auto-re-prompted; UI only reflects whether the user has tapped "Connect Health" once, with real (not boilerplate) `NSHealthShareUsageDescription` copy since App Review reads it. ActivityKit requests wrapped in try/catch that silently no-ops if Live Activities are system-disabled — no crash, no nag. `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` fired only from actual write paths (bedtime logged, check-in submitted), never from a per-minute clock tick, since the system throttles/drops excess reload calls silently.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every needed capability has a first-party Apple framework, so there's no permissive-license question to resolve — the answer is no SPM dependencies at all.

**Testing implications:** The threshold-gating test is a pure unit test against synthetic struct arrays, no `ModelContainer`, no UI — non-negotiable per app_features. Session-pairing/edge-case logic (16-hour window, orphan-closing, duplicate-tap update-in-place, DST/negative-duration rejection) gets its own Swift Testing (`@Test`) suite, parameterized over a timestamp-pair matrix. The `gradient(for: Date)` function gets a direct unit test asserting band boundaries. At least one XCUITest exercises the Today state machine end-to-end (empty → asleep → done-for-today), plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait, since that's the kind of thing that silently regresses. No ML/AR justification needed beyond what's settled — rule-based statistics is the right, honest, battery-safe choice; that question is closed.

**One additional concrete decision locked this round:** entitlement status resolves once per app-foreground into an in-memory `@Observable` flag, not re-queried from `Transaction.currentEntitlements` on every view body evaluation — re-running that async sequence inside a computed property risks a real UI stutter on Insights, the one screen that most needs to feel instantaneous.
phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Ios Architecture Review

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:31:22._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Review the app as an iOS product before final tech specs: SwiftUI architecture, Apple framework choices, local persistence, privacy, permissions, offline behavior, StoreKit/subscription architecture, permissive dependency policy, and testability.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (QA & Risk · Closer) — Round 1**

Coming at this purely from "what breaks in the field and how would we catch it before App Review does," and I want to close out every architecture question this phase is supposed to answer rather than leave anything soft for tech_specs to improvise.

On SwiftUI architecture: this should be `@Observable` view models (iOS 17+, not the older `ObservableObject`/Combine pattern — no reason to carry that weight), with two repository protocols already named in prior phases, `SleepSessionRepository` and `CheckInRepository`, injected via `@Environment` so the SwiftData-backed implementation can be swapped for an in-memory fake in tests and later for CloudKit without touching a single view. The correlation engine has to be a real Swift `actor`, not just "an async function" — that's the only way to guarantee the off-main-actor computation the design_handoff phase already committed to is actually data-race-free rather than accidentally safe today and broken the moment someone adds a second call site. Critically, the correlation engine's input type should be plain structs (arrays of session/check-in value types), not `@Model` SwiftData objects passed directly in — that's what makes the threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) actually unit-testable without spinning up a `ModelContainer` for every test run. If the engine takes SwiftData model objects as its argument type, someone will "simplify" it later by querying inside the engine, and then it's no longer a pure function you can test with synthetic data, which is exactly the failure mode app_features flagged as unacceptable.

Framework choices, stated plainly so nobody has to re-derive them: SwiftData for persistence (two `@Model` types, `SleepSession` and `CheckIn`, with `SleepSession.endDate` as `Date?` so the open-session predicate `endDate == nil` is a real, indexable query rather than a hand-rolled check); StoreKit 2 with a `.storekit` config for local testing, `Transaction.updates` listened from app launch, and `Transaction.currentEntitlements` as the source of truth rather than caching a bool in UserDefaults that can drift; ActivityKit for the Live Activity; WidgetKit for the widget; HealthKit read-only, gated entirely behind a Settings-initiated opt-in, never requested at first launch; no UserNotifications framework at all, consistent with the locked "no push/local reminders" decision — I'd make that omission explicit here so nobody adds a notification "just for the Live Activity" later. `Charts` is optional — I'd actually argue against it for two bars, hand-rolled `Capsule` views are simpler and match the frosted-card aesthetic better, and it's one less framework surface to test.

Persistence plan and the risk I want closed now: SwiftData needs an explicit versioned schema (`VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan`) from the very first commit, even though there's only one version today. The reason this can't wait: this app's whole value proposition is "unlimited history" for paying users, meaning real users will accumulate months of data before a schema change ever needs to ship, and retrofitting a migration plan onto a store that was never versioned is a much worse day than declaring one now for near-zero cost. I want this named as a hard requirement, not a nice-to-have.

Permissions and privacy, closing the ambiguous cases: HealthKit's read-authorization status is deliberately indistinguishable between "denied" and "not determined" by Apple's own design — the app must never try to infer which one happened and must never re-prompt automatically; the only UI difference should be whether the user has visited Settings and tapped "Connect Health" once, and denial (however it happened) simply keeps that toggle off forever until the user goes to iOS Settings themselves. `NSHealthShareUsageDescription` needs real, specific copy, not boilerplate, since App Review reads it. For ActivityKit: Live Activities can be globally or per-app disabled by the user in system Settings, and requesting one while disabled throws — this needs a try/catch that silently no-ops back to "no Live Activity, no error shown," not a crash and not a nag banner asking the user to go re-enable it. For WidgetKit: `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is throttled by the system if called too often — if it's wired to fire on every `TimelineView` minute tick instead of only on actual write paths (bedtime logged, wake+check-in submitted), iOS will start silently dropping reload requests for the rest of the day, which is a worse failure than never reloading at all because it looks like an intermittent bug instead of a design mistake. I want that constraint written down explicitly so the build doesn't wire it to the wrong trigger.

Dependency and license decision: zero third-party packages. Every capability this app needs — persistence, subscriptions, background activity, health read access, sharing, charts-equivalent bars — has a first-party Apple framework, so there's no permissive-license question to resolve; the answer is "no SPM dependencies at all," and I'd lock that as the decision rather than leaving it as "prefer permissive licenses if we need one," since we don't need one.

Testing implications, and this is where I want to be strict rather than aspirational: the correlation engine's threshold-gating test is non-negotiable and must run as a pure unit test against synthetic struct arrays — no `ModelContainer`, no UI, just N-1 nights in and assert zero scored insights, N nights in and assert at least one. Session-pairing logic (16-hour plausibility window, orphan-closing, duplicate-bedtime-tap update-in-place, DST/negative-duration rejection) needs its own unit test suite independent of any view, using Swift Testing (`@Test`, iOS 17+) rather than XCTest for the new code, since it reads cleaner for parameterized edge-case tables (feed a matrix of bedtime/wake timestamp pairs including a DST-fallback pair and assert which ones get discarded). The `gradient(for: Date) -> [Color]` function from design_handoff needs a direct unit test asserting band boundaries land where specified — that's cheap and it's the one visual requirement that's actually testable without a simulator. For UI-level testing, I'd want at least one XCUITest exercising the Today state machine end-to-end (empty → bedtime logged → asleep → wake+check-in → done-for-today) since that's the one screen where a state-transition bug would be immediately embarrassing, plus one accessibility-focused test that asserts the `EnergyScaleControl` actually exposes an accessibility value and adjustable trait, since that's exactly the kind of thing that silently regresses when someone touches the view later and nobody notices because Xcode's canvas doesn't surface it. I don't think this app needs any ML or AR justification beyond what's already settled — rule-based statistics is the right, honest, battery-safe choice, and I'd close that question rather than leave it open for a future phase to re-litigate.

One more concrete decision I want locked here rather than left soft: entitlement checks should resolve once per app-foreground into an in-memory `@Observable` flag, not be re-queried from `Transaction.currentEntitlements` on every view body evaluation — that async sequence isn't cheap to iterate repeatedly, and re-running it inside a SwiftUI view's computed property is a realistic way to introduce a genuine UI stutter on the Insights screen, which is precisely the screen that most needs to feel calm and instantaneous.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

CONSENSUS: YES

Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, decisive pass that closes out every architecture question this phase needs answered, and it doesn't fight anything locked in the seven prior phases — it just makes concrete, buildable calls on things design_handoff left as engineering-level detail. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it's specific enough to hand straight to tech_specs.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** `@Observable` view models (iOS 17+, not `ObservableObject`/Combine). Two repository protocols — `SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository` — injected via `@Environment`, so SwiftData can be swapped for an in-memory fake in tests and later for CloudKit without touching views. The correlation engine is a real Swift `actor` (not just "an async function") so the off-main-actor guarantee design_handoff already committed to is actually data-race-safe, not accidentally safe today. Critically, the engine's input type is plain structs (arrays of session/check-in value types), never `@Model` objects directly — that's what keeps the threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) unit-testable without a `ModelContainer`, and prevents someone from "simplifying" it later by querying SwiftData inside the engine.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftData (two `@Model` types, `SleepSession` with `endDate: Date?` for a real `endDate == nil` predicate, and `CheckIn`); StoreKit 2 (`.storekit` config for local testing, `Transaction.updates` listened from launch, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` as source of truth — never a cached bool); ActivityKit for the Live Activity; WidgetKit for the widget; HealthKit read-only, opt-in only from Settings, never requested at first launch; explicitly no UserNotifications framework anywhere, consistent with the locked no-push decision; no Charts — hand-rolled `Capsule` bars instead, matching the frosted-card aesthetic and cutting a framework surface to test.

**Persistence plan:** A versioned SwiftData schema (`VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan`) from the first commit, even with only one version today — non-negotiable given "unlimited history" is the paid value prop, so real users will accumulate months of data before any future schema change, and retrofitting migration onto an unversioned store later is far worse than declaring one now.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** HealthKit denied-vs-not-determined is indistinguishable by design — never inferred or auto-re-prompted; UI only reflects whether the user has tapped "Connect Health" once, with real (not boilerplate) `NSHealthShareUsageDescription` copy since App Review reads it. ActivityKit requests wrapped in try/catch that silently no-ops if Live Activities are system-disabled — no crash, no nag. `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` fired only from actual write paths (bedtime logged, check-in submitted), never from a per-minute clock tick, since the system throttles/drops excess reload calls silently.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every needed capability has a first-party Apple framework, so there's no permissive-license question to resolve — the answer is no SPM dependencies at all.

**Testing implications:** The threshold-gating test is a pure unit test against synthetic struct arrays, no `ModelContainer`, no UI — non-negotiable per app_features. Session-pairing/edge-case logic (16-hour window, orphan-closing, duplicate-tap update-in-place, DST/negative-duration rejection) gets its own Swift Testing (`@Test`) suite, parameterized over a timestamp-pair matrix. The `gradient(for: Date)` function gets a direct unit test asserting band boundaries. At least one XCUITest exercises the Today state machine end-to-end (empty → asleep → done-for-today), plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait, since that's the kind of thing that silently regresses. No ML/AR justification needed beyond what's settled — rule-based statistics is the right, honest, battery-safe choice; that question is closed.

**One additional concrete decision locked this round:** entitlement status resolves once per app-foreground into an in-memory `@Observable` flag, not re-queried from `Transaction.currentEntitlements` on every view body evaluation — re-running that async sequence inside a computed property risks a real UI stutter on Insights, the one screen that most needs to feel instantaneous.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. All six required outputs are present, concrete, and consistent with the seven prior locked phases and the global rubric:

- **SwiftUI architecture**: Specific and unambiguous — `@Observable` view models, named repository protocols (`SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository`) injected via `@Environment` for testability and future CloudKit swap-in, correlation engine as a real `actor` taking plain structs (not `@Model` objects) so the mandatory threshold-gating test stays a pure unit test. This closes a real gap: design_handoff said "async off-main-actor" but didn't specify the concurrency primitive or input type, both of which matter for whether the non-negotiable threshold test is actually writable.
- **Apple framework choices**: Concrete and fully mapped to needs (SwiftData, StoreKit 2, ActivityKit, WidgetKit, HealthKit read-only), with an explicit, reasoned omission list (no UserNotifications, no Charts) rather than silence.
- **Persistence plan**: Versioned schema from commit one, justified against the specific product fact that unlimited history is the paid feature — this is exactly the kind of forward-looking call this phase exists to make before tech_specs locks implementation.
- **Permissions/privacy plan**: Correctly handles the HealthKit denied-vs-not-determined ambiguity (a real iOS API footgun), ActivityKit disabled-state fail-safe, and the WidgetKit reload-throttling constraint with a specific correct trigger (write paths only, not clock ticks) — all consistent with the privacy/degrade-gracefully commitments locked in detailed_discussion and design_handoff.
- **Dependency/license decision**: Clean, decisive — zero SPM dependencies, satisfying the acceptance check trivially and correctly (no dependency to evaluate against the permissive-license policy).
- **Testing implications**: Directly preserves the app_features "must-have test" (threshold-gating N-1/N) word-for-word in spirit, adds a parameterized edge-case suite for session-pairing, a unit test for the gradient band function from design_handoff, and named UI/accessibility tests. ML/AR question is explicitly closed with justification (rule-based stats is the honest, battery-safe choice), satisfying that acceptance check directly.

Acceptance checks satisfied: no vague architecture gaps remain (every framework, data type, and concurrency choice is named), ML use is justified by user value and not over-scoped, and no dependency violates the permissive-license policy (there are none). The one new decision this round — entitlement resolved once per foreground into an in-memory flag rather than re-queried per view-body-evaluation — is a good, specific catch that prevents a plausible Insights-screen stutter. Nothing here contradicts prior phases; it operationalizes them. Ready to close and hand to tech_specs.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

CONSENSUS: YES

Only Claude showed up again this round (Codex still in cooldown), but it's a complete, decisive pass that closes out every architecture question this phase needs answered, and it doesn't fight anything locked in the seven prior phases — it just makes concrete, buildable calls on things design_handoff left as engineering-level detail. Nothing here is contested since it's a solo pass, and it's specific enough to hand straight to tech_specs.

## Final Output

**SwiftUI architecture:** `@Observable` view models (iOS 17+, not `ObservableObject`/Combine). Two repository protocols — `SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository` — injected via `@Environment`, so SwiftData can be swapped for an in-memory fake in tests and later for CloudKit without touching views. The correlation engine is a real Swift `actor` (not just "an async function") so the off-main-actor guarantee design_handoff already committed to is actually data-race-safe, not accidentally safe today. Critically, the engine's input type is plain structs (arrays of session/check-in value types), never `@Model` objects directly — that's what keeps the threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero insights, N nights → at least one) unit-testable without a `ModelContainer`, and prevents someone from "simplifying" it later by querying SwiftData inside the engine.

**Apple framework choices:** SwiftData (two `@Model` types, `SleepSession` with `endDate: Date?` for a real `endDate == nil` predicate, and `CheckIn`); StoreKit 2 (`.storekit` config for local testing, `Transaction.updates` listened from launch, `Transaction.currentEntitlements` as source of truth — never a cached bool); ActivityKit for the Live Activity; WidgetKit for the widget; HealthKit read-only, opt-in only from Settings, never requested at first launch; explicitly no UserNotifications framework anywhere, consistent with the locked no-push decision; no Charts — hand-rolled `Capsule` bars instead, matching the frosted-card aesthetic and cutting a framework surface to test.

**Persistence plan:** A versioned SwiftData schema (`VersionedSchema`/`SchemaMigrationPlan`) from the first commit, even with only one version today — non-negotiable given "unlimited history" is the paid value prop, so real users will accumulate months of data before any future schema change, and retrofitting migration onto an unversioned store later is far worse than declaring one now.

**Permissions/privacy plan:** HealthKit denied-vs-not-determined is indistinguishable by design — never inferred or auto-re-prompted; UI only reflects whether the user has tapped "Connect Health" once, with real (not boilerplate) `NSHealthShareUsageDescription` copy since App Review reads it. ActivityKit requests wrapped in try/catch that silently no-ops if Live Activities are system-disabled — no crash, no nag. `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` fired only from actual write paths (bedtime logged, check-in submitted), never from a per-minute clock tick, since the system throttles/drops excess reload calls silently.

**Dependency/license decision:** Zero third-party packages. Every needed capability has a first-party Apple framework, so there's no permissive-license question to resolve — the answer is no SPM dependencies at all.

**Testing implications:** The threshold-gating test is a pure unit test against synthetic struct arrays, no `ModelContainer`, no UI — non-negotiable per app_features. Session-pairing/edge-case logic (16-hour window, orphan-closing, duplicate-tap update-in-place, DST/negative-duration rejection) gets its own Swift Testing (`@Test`) suite, parameterized over a timestamp-pair matrix. The `gradient(for: Date)` function gets a direct unit test asserting band boundaries. At least one XCUITest exercises the Today state machine end-to-end (empty → asleep → done-for-today), plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait, since that's the kind of thing that silently regresses. No ML/AR justification needed beyond what's settled — rule-based statistics is the right, honest, battery-safe choice; that question is closed.

**One additional concrete decision locked this round:** entitlement status resolves once per app-foreground into an in-memory `@Observable` flag, not re-queried from `Transaction.currentEntitlements` on every view body evaluation — re-running that async sequence inside a computed property risks a real UI stutter on Insights, the one screen that most needs to feel instantaneous.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.title: Tech Specs
phase_discussions.output:
Round 1 landed the same way every prior phase has: Codex stayed unavailable, so it's a single thorough pass, but this one closes out every required tech-specs output cleanly and doesn't contradict anything locked across the eight prior phases — it just types out what design_handoff and ios_architecture_review already decided. The one real judgment call this round adds is making the correlation engine an open `InsightRule` protocol with exactly two registered implementations rather than a hardcoded if/else, defended narrowly (two structs implementing a 3-line protocol, not a plugin system) on the grounds that "per-tag correlations, plural" is the literal paid-tier pitch, not a hypothetical future need — that's a reasonable amount of care for the one component the whole product's differentiation rests on, and it costs nothing extra to build now. Everything else (SwiftData model shapes, repository protocols, actor-based engine, pure `NightRecord` value-type test seam, versioned schema, StoreKit/entitlement handling, testing plan) is a direct, concrete translation of what ios_architecture_review already locked. Nothing here is contested, and it satisfies both acceptance checks — parallel build lanes have a full shared type contract to code against, and the spec names exactly how the app gets built and verified.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Single Xcode project (`Somna.xcodeproj`), three targets — `Somna` (app), `SomnaWidget` (WidgetKit extension, also hosts the Live Activity), `SomnaTests` (Swift Testing unit tests). No SPM-local-package split; one app target with clean internal folders gives full testability without build-graph overhead. `@Observable` view models throughout (iOS 17+, no Combine). Two repository protocols (`SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository`) injected at the View layer via `@Environment` and passed into view-model initializers as concrete protocol instances — view models never pull `@Environment` themselves, so every view model is constructible in tests with in-memory fakes. The correlation engine is a real `actor` operating only on plain `NightRecord` structs, never SwiftData `@Model` objects, which is what keeps the non-negotiable threshold-gating test (N‑1 nights → zero scored insights, N nights → at least one) a pure unit test with no `ModelContainer`. Zero third-party dependencies — every capability (persistence, subscriptions, background activity, health read access, sharing, comparison bars) has a first-party Apple framework.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `@main` App struct, scene setup, root `TabView`.
- `Models/` — the two `@Model` types plus plain-struct value types the engine consumes.
- `Repositories/` — `SleepSessionRepository`/`CheckInRepository` protocols; `Repositories/SwiftData/` for the real implementations; in-memory fakes live in the test target.
- `Engine/` — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, `InsightResult`/`InsightCardData`, tag/energy enums, `NightRecordAssembler`, and the pure `gradient(for:)` function (treated as derived-state logic even though it feeds visuals).
- `ViewModels/` — one `@Observable` class per screen: `TodayViewModel`, `InsightsViewModel`, `HistoryViewModel`, `RecapViewModel`, `PaywallViewModel`.
- `Views/` — grouped by screen per design_handoff's component list (`Today/`, `Insights/`, `History/`, `Recap/`, `Settings/`, `Paywall/`, `Components/` for `FrostedCard`/`InsightCard`/`EnergyScaleControl`/etc.).
- `Subscriptions/` — StoreKit manager + entitlement store.
- `HealthKit/` — optional import service, isolated so it's trivially excludable from anything that doesn't want to link HealthKit.
- `Live Activity/` — `ActivityAttributes` struct marked as belonging to both the app and widget extension targets (lightweight shared-file pattern, not a separate framework).

**Data models:** `SleepSession` (`@Model`: `id`, `startDate`, `endDate: Date?` — open/closed is a real `#Predicate(endDate == nil)` at the query site, never a hand-reconstructed check). `CheckIn` (`@Model`: `id`, `timestamp`, `energyLevel: Int` 1–5 wrapped by `EnergyLevel` enum at all boundaries, `tagsRaw: [String]` storing `SleepTag` raw values — enums-as-strings deliberately chosen over a join table since the tag set is fixed and small, `linkedSessionID: UUID?` as a soft foreign key, not a SwiftData relationship, so the 16-hour pairing/orphaning rule stays explicit application logic rather than implicit cascade behavior). `NightRecord` is the pure value-type boundary the engine actually consumes, built by the pure function `NightRecordAssembler.assemble(...)`, which pairs sessions to check-ins within the plausibility window, orphans the rest, and discards implausible/negative durations before a record is ever created — the engine itself never touches SwiftData.

**Persistence strategy:** `ModelContainer` configured with `SomnaSchemaV1: VersionedSchema` and an (initially empty) `SomnaMigrationPlan` from the first commit — non-negotiable given unlimited history is the paid feature. Repositories wrap `ModelContext` behind the two protocols; `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is called exclusively from the repository write methods (`startOrUpdateSession`, `endSession`, `submit`), one call site each, so nothing can accidentally wire it to a clock tick.

**Testing strategy:** `SomnaTests` needs zero HealthKit/StoreKit linkage to run fast. `CorrelationEngineTests` run purely on `[NightRecord]` literals and assert the threshold-gating contract word-for-word. `SessionPairingTests` use a parameterized `@Test` matrix (16h-boundary pair, DST-fallback pair, duplicate-tap pair). `GradientTests` assert band boundaries from `gradient(for:)`. `TodayStateMachineTests` drive `TodayViewModel.state(at:)` through empty→asleep→done using in-memory repositories. One XCUITest smoke-tests the real UI end-to-end since no unit test touches actual rendering, plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait.

```interfaces-json

Queried via #Predicate, never a hand-reconstructed check."},
  {"name": "CheckIn", "kind": "struct", "language": "swift", "signature": "@Model final class CheckIn { var id: UUID; var timestamp: Date; var energyLevel: Int; var tagsRaw: [String]; var linkedSessionID: UUID? }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "energyLevel stored 1-5, wrap with EnergyLevel enum at boundaries. tagsRaw stores SleepTag.rawValue strings. linkedSessionID is a soft FK set by pairing logic, not a SwiftData relationship."},
  {"name": "EnergyLevel", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum EnergyLevel: Int, CaseIterable { case exhausted = 1, low, okay, good, rested; var label: String { get } }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Backs EnergyScaleControl and all display copy; VoiceOver spoken value comes from .label."},
  {"name": "SleepTag", "kind": "enum", "language": "swift", "signature": "enum SleepTag: String, CaseIterable, Codable { case caffeine, exercise, screenTime, stress, alcohol }", "owning_lane": "data_domain", "notes": "Fixed starter tag set. Consumed by InsightCard(isLocked:) which blurs comparisonA/B bars when isPremium && !entitled."},
```
