phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Tech Specs

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:33:05._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

**Claude (Frontend Engineer · Visionary) — Round 1**

Picking up architecture that's already 90% decided by seven rounds of upstream discussion — my job this round is to stop describing and start typing, and to push where I think the build can be more ambitious than "safe MVP" without breaking anything already locked.

The app is a single Xcode project, `Somna.xcodeproj`, with three targets: `Somna` (the app), `SomnaWidget` (WidgetKit extension, also hosting the Live Activity), and `SomnaTests` (Swift Testing unit tests) — no separate framework/package split, because this app is small enough that a modularized SPM-local-package layout would be pure ceremony; a single app target with clean internal folders gets us the same testability without the build-graph overhead. Folder layout inside the app target: `App/` (the `@main` App struct, scene setup, root TabView), `Models/` (the two `@Model` types plus the plain-struct value types the correlation engine consumes), `Repositories/` (the two protocols plus their SwiftData-backed and in-memory implementations), `Engine/` (the `CorrelationEngine` actor, its input/output types, the tag/energy enums, and the pure `gradient(for:)` function since it's conceptually "derived state from data" even though it's visual), `ViewModels/` (one `@Observable` class per screen — `TodayViewModel`, `InsightsViewModel`, `HistoryViewModel`, `RecapViewModel`, `PaywallViewModel`), `Views/` (grouped by screen, matching the design_handoff component list — `Today/`, `Insights/`, `History/`, `Recap/`, `Settings/`, `Paywall/`, and `Components/` for the shared `FrostedCard`/`InsightCard`/`EnergyScaleControl`/etc.), `Subscriptions/` (StoreKit manager + entitlement store), `HealthKit/` (the optional import service, isolated so it's trivially excludable from a test target that doesn't want to link HealthKit), and `Live Activity/` shared attribute types that both the app and widget extension import via a small shared source-file group (not a full framework — just marking the ActivityAttributes struct as belonging to both targets in the file inspector, which is the standard lightweight pattern for this).

Data model: `SleepSession` is a SwiftData `@Model` with `id: UUID`, `startDate: Date`, `endDate: Date?`, and a computed `isOpen` that's really backed by a real `#Predicate` (`endDate == nil`) at the query site, not a computed property masquerading as one. `CheckIn` is a SwiftData `@Model` with `id: UUID`, `timestamp: Date`, `energyLevel: Int` (1–5, wrapped by an `EnergyLevel` enum at the view/engine boundary so nobody works with raw ints in UI code), `tags: [String]` (raw values of a `SleepTag` enum, stored as strings for schema stability — enums-as-raw-storage is the one place I'd deliberately avoid over-engineering with a separate join table; a fixed tag set doesn't need relational modeling), and `linkedSessionID: UUID?` for the pairing relationship, deliberately a soft foreign key rather than a SwiftData relationship — I want pairing logic (16-hour window, orphaning) to be explicit application logic operating on plain data, not something a relationship's delete-rule/cascade behavior does implicitly and surprisingly.

Where I want to push harder than what's been agreed so far: the correlation engine shouldn't just be "two hardcoded insight types," even though that's what's locked — I think the engine itself should be architected as a small, open `InsightRule` protocol from day one (`func evaluate(nights: [NightRecord]) -> InsightResult?`), with exactly two concrete implementations shipped (`DurationEnergyRule`, `TagEnergyRule(tag: .caffeine)`) registered in an array the actor iterates. This costs nothing extra to build now, and it means the "narrow scope, two insight types" decision that was rightly made for time reasons doesn't calcify into a hardcoded if/else that has to be torn up the moment someone wants a third rule post-launch — it's the difference between an insight engine and an insight function with a rope around its neck. I'd defend this as the one place in the build worth spending a little extra architectural care, precisely because this app's entire pitch is that the insight engine is the product; if the engine's internals are a dead-end special case, we've under-built the one thing that's supposed to be remarkable.

`NightRecord` is the plain struct the engine actually consumes — `sessionDuration: TimeInterval?`, `energyLevel: EnergyLevel`, `tags: Set<SleepTag>`, `date: Date` — built by a `NightRecordAssembler` (a pure function, not a repository method) that joins `SleepSession`+`CheckIn` value snapshots into these records, discarding implausible/negative durations right there per the locked data-integrity rule, so the engine itself never has to know about SwiftData, `@Model`, discard rules, or the 16-hour pairing window — it just gets a clean array of nights, some with `nil` duration (standalone check-ins), and its threshold-gating and rule logic operate purely on that.

Persistence: `ModelContainer` configured with a `SomnaSchemaV1: VersionedSchema` and an empty `SomnaMigrationPlan` (one stage today, ready to append). Repositories wrap `ModelContext` operations behind `SleepSessionRepository`/`CheckInRepository` protocols; the concrete SwiftData implementations live in `Repositories/SwiftData/`, and a `InMemoryRepository` pair lives under the test target for fast, no-disk unit tests of view models. `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines(ofKind:)` is called exclusively from the repository's write methods (`startSession`, `endSession`, `submitCheckIn`) — one call site each, not sprinkled through view code, so nobody can accidentally wire it to a clock tick.

State management: `@Observable` view models own their screen's derived state and hold repository references via initializer injection (not property wrappers pulling from environment inside the view model itself — environment injection happens at the View layer, and the view model gets concrete protocol instances passed in, which keeps view models trivially constructible in tests without a live `@Environment`). `TodayViewModel` is the one with real complexity: it exposes a single `enum TodayState { case empty, preBedtime(Summary), asleep(ElapsedInfo), doneForToday(NightSummary) }` computed from the current open session + today's check-in + `Date.now`, fed by a `TimelineView(.everyMinute)` at the view layer that passes `context.date` into the view model's `state(at:)` function — meaning the state derivation itself is a pure, testable function of (session, check-in, now), not something living inside a SwiftUI `body`.

For the paywall/entitlement layer, `SubscriptionStore` is an `@Observable` singleton-ish object (owned by the App struct, injected via environment) that resolves `Transaction.currentEntitlements` once per scenePhase-active transition into a plain `EntitlementStatus` enum (`.free`, `.subscribed`, `.unknown`) — `.unknown` is a deliberate third state I want preserved, distinct from `.free`, so a load failure can render "checking..." for at most a brief moment while still treating gated features as locked-but-not-blocking rather than either prematurely unlocking or hard-blocking Today.

On testing: the `SomnaTests` target needs zero HealthKit/StoreKit linkage to run fast — `CorrelationEngineTests` operate purely on `[NightRecord]` literals, `SessionPairingTests` operate on a parameterized `@Test` matrix of timestamp pairs (including a DST-fall-back pair, an over-16-hour pair, a duplicate-tap pair), `GradientTests` assert band boundaries from `gradient(for:)`, and one `TodayStateMachineTests` suite drives `TodayViewModel.state(at:)` through the full empty→asleep→done transition using the in-memory repositories. A single XCUITest smoke-tests the real UI end-to-end since none of the unit tests touch actual view rendering.

The biggest risk to my own proposal: the `InsightRule` protocol abstraction is technically more than what's strictly needed for "two hardcoded rule types," and someone reviewing for scope discipline could reasonably call it premature generalization given this codebase's own stated aversion to speculative abstraction. I'd defend it narrowly — it's two structs implementing one three-line protocol, not a plugin system, and it's the one part of this app where "what if we need a third rule" isn't hypothetical, it's the explicitly stated subscription pitch (per-tag correlations, plural, per the spec's "caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags"). The detail I most want preserved into the final spec: `NightRecord` as the clean value-type boundary between persistence and engine, and the `InsightRule` protocol with exactly two registered implementations — both cost nothing today and prevent the correlation engine from becoming unextendable exactly where the product's paid tier depends on it being extended.

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Single Xcode project (`Somna.xcodeproj`), three targets — `Somna` (app), `SomnaWidget` (WidgetKit extension, also hosts the Live Activity), `SomnaTests` (Swift Testing unit tests). No SPM-local-package split; one app target with clean internal folders gives full testability without build-graph overhead. `@Observable` view models throughout (iOS 17+, no Combine). Two repository protocols (`SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository`) injected at the View layer via `@Environment` and passed into view-model initializers as concrete protocol instances — view models never pull `@Environment` themselves, so every view model is constructible in tests with in-memory fakes. The correlation engine is a real `actor` operating only on plain `NightRecord` structs, never SwiftData `@Model` objects, which is what keeps the non-negotiable threshold-gating test (N‑1 nights → zero scored insights, N nights → at least one) a pure unit test with no `ModelContainer`. Zero third-party dependencies — every capability (persistence, subscriptions, background activity, health read access, sharing, comparison bars) has a first-party Apple framework.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `@main` App struct, scene setup, root `TabView`.
- `Models/` — the two `@Model` types plus plain-struct value types the engine consumes.
- `Repositories/` — `SleepSessionRepository`/`CheckInRepository` protocols; `Repositories/SwiftData/` for the real implementations; in-memory fakes live in the test target.
- `Engine/` — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, `InsightResult`/`InsightCardData`, tag/energy enums, `NightRecordAssembler`, and the pure `gradient(for:)` function (treated as derived-state logic even though it feeds visuals).
- `ViewModels/` — one `@Observable` class per screen: `TodayViewModel`, `InsightsViewModel`, `HistoryViewModel`, `RecapViewModel`, `PaywallViewModel`.
- `Views/` — grouped by screen per design_handoff's component list (`Today/`, `Insights/`, `History/`, `Recap/`, `Settings/`, `Paywall/`, `Components/` for `FrostedCard`/`InsightCard`/`EnergyScaleControl`/etc.).
- `Subscriptions/` — StoreKit manager + entitlement store.
- `HealthKit/` — optional import service, isolated so it's trivially excludable from anything that doesn't want to link HealthKit.
- `Live Activity/` — `ActivityAttributes` struct marked as belonging to both the app and widget extension targets (lightweight shared-file pattern, not a separate framework).

**Data models:** `SleepSession` (`@Model`: `id`, `startDate`, `endDate: Date?` — open/closed is a real `#Predicate(endDate == nil)` at the query site, never a hand-reconstructed check). `CheckIn` (`@Model`: `id`, `timestamp`, `energyLevel: Int` 1–5 wrapped by `EnergyLevel` enum at all boundaries, `tagsRaw: [String]` storing `SleepTag` raw values — enums-as-strings deliberately chosen over a join table since the tag set is fixed and small, `linkedSessionID: UUID?` as a soft foreign key, not a SwiftData relationship, so the 16-hour pairing/orphaning rule stays explicit application logic rather than implicit cascade behavior). `NightRecord` is the pure value-type boundary the engine actually consumes, built by the pure function `NightRecordAssembler.assemble(...)`, which pairs sessions to check-ins within the plausibility window, orphans the rest, and discards implausible/negative durations before a record is ever created — the engine itself never touches SwiftData.

**Persistence strategy:** `ModelContainer` configured with `SomnaSchemaV1: VersionedSchema` and an (initially empty) `SomnaMigrationPlan` from the first commit — non-negotiable given unlimited history is the paid feature. Repositories wrap `ModelContext` behind the two protocols; `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is called exclusively from the repository write methods (`startOrUpdateSession`, `endSession`, `submit`), one call site each, so nothing can accidentally wire it to a clock tick.

**Testing strategy:** `SomnaTests` needs zero HealthKit/StoreKit linkage to run fast. `CorrelationEngineTests` run purely on `[NightRecord]` literals and assert the threshold-gating contract word-for-word. `SessionPairingTests` use a parameterized `@Test` matrix (16h-boundary pair, DST-fallback pair, duplicate-tap pair). `GradientTests` assert band boundaries from `gradient(for:)`. `TodayStateMachineTests` drive `TodayViewModel.state(at:)` through empty→asleep→done using in-memory repositories. One XCUITest smoke-tests the real UI end-to-end since no unit test touches actual rendering, plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait.

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
Passes. This is a complete, concrete tech spec that operationalizes the eight prior locked phases into a buildable architecture without contradicting any of them.

- **Architecture overview**: Single Xcode project, three targets (app/widget-extension/tests), `@Observable` view models, repository-protocol injection at the View layer (never `@Environment` inside view models — keeps them test-constructible), correlation engine as a real `actor` over plain `NightRecord` structs. Zero third-party dependencies, consistent with the locked dependency policy.
- **File/module layout**: Concrete folder structure (`App/`, `Models/`, `Repositories/` with `SwiftData/` subfolder and test-target in-memory fakes, `Engine/`, `ViewModels/`, `Views/` grouped by screen, `Subscriptions/`, `HealthKit/`, `Live Activity/`) — a builder could scaffold this directly.
- **Data models**: `SleepSession`/`CheckIn` as `@Model` types with the `endDate == nil` real-predicate open-session rule, `linkedSessionID` as a deliberate soft FK (not a SwiftData relationship) so the 16-hour pairing/orphaning logic stays explicit application code — directly honors the `detailed_discussion` and `ios_architecture_review` decisions. `NightRecord` as the pure value-type test seam is the correct mechanism for keeping the non-negotiable threshold-gating test a `ModelContainer`-free unit test.
- **Persistence strategy**: Versioned schema from commit one, repository write-methods as the sole `WidgetCenter.reloadTimelines` call sites — matches the locked "never wired to a clock tick" requirement.
- **Testing strategy**: Directly names the threshold-gating test, parameterized session-pairing edge cases (DST, 16h boundary, duplicate-tap), gradient band test, Today state-machine test, and an accessibility test for `EnergyScaleControl` — all previously-locked non-negotiables are explicitly present, not implied.
- **interfaces-json**: Present, well-formed, one JSON object with an `interfaces` array. Every entry has `name`, `kind`, `language`, `signature`, `owning_lane`, `notes`; all `owning_lane` values are valid (`data_domain`, `primary_ui`, `services_utilities`, `polish_resilience`). Coverage is genuinely cross-lane and complete: data models, repositories, the `InsightRule`/`CorrelationEngine` engine layer, all view models, StoreKit/entitlement types, HealthKit service, Live Activity/Widget types, and shared projection types (`NightSummary`, `RecapData`) that prevent lane drift.

The one new judgment call this round — an open `InsightRule` protocol with exactly two registered implementations rather than a hardcoded branch — is well-justified (near-zero cost, matches the literal plural "per-tag correlations" paid-tier pitch) and doesn't violate the anti-overengineering rule since it's two structs against a 3-line protocol, not a plugin system.

Acceptance checks satisfied: parallel build lanes (data_domain, primary_ui, services_utilities, polish_resilience) have a complete, non-contradictory shared type contract to code against, and the spec names concretely how the app is built (file layout, frameworks, patterns) and verified (specific unit/UI test suite). Ready to close and move to build.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Round 1 landed the same way every prior phase has: Codex stayed unavailable, so it's a single thorough pass, but this one closes out every required tech-specs output cleanly and doesn't contradict anything locked across the eight prior phases — it just types out what design_handoff and ios_architecture_review already decided. The one real judgment call this round adds is making the correlation engine an open `InsightRule` protocol with exactly two registered implementations rather than a hardcoded if/else, defended narrowly (two structs implementing a 3-line protocol, not a plugin system) on the grounds that "per-tag correlations, plural" is the literal paid-tier pitch, not a hypothetical future need — that's a reasonable amount of care for the one component the whole product's differentiation rests on, and it costs nothing extra to build now. Everything else (SwiftData model shapes, repository protocols, actor-based engine, pure `NightRecord` value-type test seam, versioned schema, StoreKit/entitlement handling, testing plan) is a direct, concrete translation of what ios_architecture_review already locked. Nothing here is contested, and it satisfies both acceptance checks — parallel build lanes have a full shared type contract to code against, and the spec names exactly how the app gets built and verified.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Architecture overview:** Single Xcode project (`Somna.xcodeproj`), three targets — `Somna` (app), `SomnaWidget` (WidgetKit extension, also hosts the Live Activity), `SomnaTests` (Swift Testing unit tests). No SPM-local-package split; one app target with clean internal folders gives full testability without build-graph overhead. `@Observable` view models throughout (iOS 17+, no Combine). Two repository protocols (`SleepSessionRepository`, `CheckInRepository`) injected at the View layer via `@Environment` and passed into view-model initializers as concrete protocol instances — view models never pull `@Environment` themselves, so every view model is constructible in tests with in-memory fakes. The correlation engine is a real `actor` operating only on plain `NightRecord` structs, never SwiftData `@Model` objects, which is what keeps the non-negotiable threshold-gating test (N‑1 nights → zero scored insights, N nights → at least one) a pure unit test with no `ModelContainer`. Zero third-party dependencies — every capability (persistence, subscriptions, background activity, health read access, sharing, comparison bars) has a first-party Apple framework.

**File/module layout:**
- `App/` — `@main` App struct, scene setup, root `TabView`.
- `Models/` — the two `@Model` types plus plain-struct value types the engine consumes.
- `Repositories/` — `SleepSessionRepository`/`CheckInRepository` protocols; `Repositories/SwiftData/` for the real implementations; in-memory fakes live in the test target.
- `Engine/` — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, `InsightResult`/`InsightCardData`, tag/energy enums, `NightRecordAssembler`, and the pure `gradient(for:)` function (treated as derived-state logic even though it feeds visuals).
- `ViewModels/` — one `@Observable` class per screen: `TodayViewModel`, `InsightsViewModel`, `HistoryViewModel`, `RecapViewModel`, `PaywallViewModel`.
- `Views/` — grouped by screen per design_handoff's component list (`Today/`, `Insights/`, `History/`, `Recap/`, `Settings/`, `Paywall/`, `Components/` for `FrostedCard`/`InsightCard`/`EnergyScaleControl`/etc.).
- `Subscriptions/` — StoreKit manager + entitlement store.
- `HealthKit/` — optional import service, isolated so it's trivially excludable from anything that doesn't want to link HealthKit.
- `Live Activity/` — `ActivityAttributes` struct marked as belonging to both the app and widget extension targets (lightweight shared-file pattern, not a separate framework).

**Data models:** `SleepSession` (`@Model`: `id`, `startDate`, `endDate: Date?` — open/closed is a real `#Predicate(endDate == nil)` at the query site, never a hand-reconstructed check). `CheckIn` (`@Model`: `id`, `timestamp`, `energyLevel: Int` 1–5 wrapped by `EnergyLevel` enum at all boundaries, `tagsRaw: [String]` storing `SleepTag` raw values — enums-as-strings deliberately chosen over a join table since the tag set is fixed and small, `linkedSessionID: UUID?` as a soft foreign key, not a SwiftData relationship, so the 16-hour pairing/orphaning rule stays explicit application logic rather than implicit cascade behavior). `NightRecord` is the pure value-type boundary the engine actually consumes, built by the pure function `NightRecordAssembler.assemble(...)`, which pairs sessions to check-ins within the plausibility window, orphans the rest, and discards implausible/negative durations before a record is ever created — the engine itself never touches SwiftData.

**Persistence strategy:** `ModelContainer` configured with `SomnaSchemaV1: VersionedSchema` and an (initially empty) `SomnaMigrationPlan` from the first commit — non-negotiable given unlimited history is the paid feature. Repositories wrap `ModelContext` behind the two protocols; `WidgetCenter.shared.reloadTimelines` is called exclusively from the repository write methods (`startOrUpdateSession`, `endSession`, `submit`), one call site each, so nothing can accidentally wire it to a clock tick.

**Testing strategy:** `SomnaTests` needs zero HealthKit/StoreKit linkage to run fast. `CorrelationEngineTests` run purely on `[NightRecord]` literals and assert the threshold-gating contract word-for-word. `SessionPairingTests` use a parameterized `@Test` matrix (16h-boundary pair, DST-fallback pair, duplicate-tap pair). `GradientTests` assert band boundaries from `gradient(for:)`. `TodayStateMachineTests` drive `TodayViewModel.state(at:)` through empty→asleep→done using in-memory repositories. One XCUITest smoke-tests the real UI end-to-end since no unit test touches actual rendering, plus one accessibility test asserting `EnergyScaleControl` exposes a real accessibility value and adjustable trait.

phase_discussions.title: Project Plan
phase_discussions.output:
CONSENSUS: YES

Just the one voice this round again — Codex is still stuck in cooldown — but the Skeptic pass doesn't have a debate partner to disagree with, and there's nothing to contest here anyway: it takes the fully-locked tech_specs contract and turns it into an actual sequencing plan with teeth, rather than second-guessing anything already decided. None of this contradicts the nine prior locked phases — it's the missing "how do we actually stage and verify this" layer on top of an already-agreed architecture.

## Final Output

**Milestones (in sequence, with parallelism called out):**

1. **M0 — Scaffold everything, build clean.** All three Xcode targets (`Somna` app, `SomnaWidget` extension hosting the Live Activity, `SomnaTests`) created and building empty on day one, with signing, entitlements, and shared-file target membership (the `ActivityAttributes` struct shared between app and widget) configured now — not bolted on later when there's no slack left to debug a build-graph problem. Nothing else starts until this is green.

2. **M1a — Data-domain lane (parallel with M1b).** `SleepSession`/`CheckIn` SwiftData models, the versioned schema + migration plan *actually wired into the `ModelContainer` init* (not just declared as types), both repository protocols, their SwiftData-backed implementations, and the `InMemoryRepository` fakes — shipped together as one deliverable, not the fakes deferred, since the UI lane's parallel start depends on having something executable to build against.

3. **M1b — Engine lane (parallel with M1a).** `NightRecord`, `NightRecordAssembler`, the `CorrelationEngine` actor, both `InsightRule` implementations, and the debug/seed-data path for force-feeding synthetic nights. This milestone is not "done" until the threshold-gating test (N-1 nights → zero scored insights, N nights → at least one) exists and passes both branches — not "engine built, test pending."

4. **M2a — UI lane (parallel with M2b, depends on M1a's in-memory fakes).** Today/Insights/History/Recap/Settings views and view models, built and testable against `InMemoryRepository` before real SwiftData is wired in.

5. **M2b — Services lane (parallel with M2a, depends only on M1a's protocols).** StoreKit/`SubscriptionStore`/`PaywallViewModel`, `HealthKitImportService` — structurally isolated from the core logging/check-in screens, so this can run fully concurrent with M2a.

6. **M3 — Session-pairing edge-case suite.** 16-hour window, orphan-closing, duplicate-tap update-in-place, DST fallback — parameterized against a real pinned `TimeZone` (e.g. `America/New_York` on a known transition date), explicitly not `Calendar.current`/`TimeZone.current`, since that variant passes everywhere while testing nothing.

7. **M4 — Integration.** Swap the in-memory fakes for the real SwiftData repositories throughout the app; this is where lane-boundary assumptions (ordering, async latency) either hold or reveal themselves.

8. **M5 — Live Activity + Widget (last, degrade-gracefully scope).** Built only once Today's state machine and the `NightSummary` write path are stable, since building these earlier just means redoing them.

9. **M6 — Final verification pass.** Clean `xcodebuild` across app + widget targets on a fresh checkout; XCUITest Today-state-machine smoke test; a real manual VoiceOver pass on `EnergyScaleControl` (not just confirming the modifier exists in code); manual walkthrough of every screen's empty/loading/success/error state; a deliberately simulated StoreKit product-load failure to confirm fail-open behavior; a deliberate HealthKit permission denial to confirm full functionality with zero degradation.

**Dependencies:** M0 blocks everything. M1a and M1b are mutually independent and both block M2a/M2b (M2a needs M1a's fakes; M2b needs M1a's protocols and, for the paywall, nothing from M1b). M3 can run alongside M2a/M2b once M1a's real repos exist to test against. M4 needs M1a (real repos), M2a, and M2b all complete. M5 needs M4's stable write paths. M6 needs M4 and M5.

- In-memory fakes treated as a first deliverable, not a testing nicety — prevents the UI lane from silently blocking on the data lane.
- Threshold-gating test and seed-data path are gating criteria for their milestone's "done," not follow-up work — prevents the app's one differentiating mechanism from shipping unverified.
- DST/session-pairing tests must pin explicit timezones — prevents tests that pass without exercising anything.

- StoreKit fail-open and HealthKit denial are named explicitly as M6 checks specifically because they require deliberately breaking something rather than testing the happy path, which is exactly the kind of check that gets silently skipped otherwise.

**Verification plan:** Unit tests (engine, session-pairing, gradient bands) run as each owning milestone closes, not deferred to the end. Integration-level verification (clean multi-target build, XCUITest smoke test, manual accessibility/empty-state walkthrough, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failure paths) happens once at M6, after real repositories replace the fakes.

# somna — Project Plan

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.

8. **M5 — Live Activity + Widget (last, degrade-gracefully scope).** Built only once Today's state machine and the `NightSummary` write path are stable, since building these earlier just means redoing them.

9. **M6 — Final verification pass.** Clean `xcodebuild` across app + widget targets on a fresh checkout; XCUITest Today-state-machine smoke test; a real manual VoiceOver pass on `EnergyScaleControl` (not just confirming the modifier exists in code); manual walkthrough of every screen's empty/loading/success/error state; a deliberately simulated StoreKit product-load failure to confirm fail-open behavior; a deliberate HealthKit permission denial to confirm full functionality with zero degradation.

**Dependencies:** M0 blocks everything. M1a and M1b are mutually independent and both block M2a/M2b (M2a needs M1a's fakes; M2b needs M1a's protocols and, for the paywall, nothing from M1b). M3 can run alongside M2a/M2b once M1a's real repos exist to test against. M4 needs M1a (real repos), M2a, and M2b all complete. M5 needs M4's stable write paths. M6 needs M4 and M5.

**Risk mitigations:**
- In-memory fakes treated as a first deliverable, not a testing nicety — prevents the UI lane from silently blocking on the data lane.
- Threshold-gating test and seed-data path are gating criteria for their milestone's "done," not follow-up work — prevents the app's one differentiating mechanism from shipping unverified.
- DST/session-pairing tests must pin explicit timezones — prevents tests that pass without exercising anything.
- Versioned schema gets a named verification step (confirm the `ModelContainer` is actually configured with the migration plan), not just a code-review glance at the type declarations.
- StoreKit fail-open and HealthKit denial are named explicitly as M6 checks specifically because they require deliberately breaking something rather than testing the happy path, which is exactly the kind of check that gets silently skipped otherwise.

**Verification plan:** Unit tests (engine, session-pairing, gradient bands) run as each owning milestone closes, not deferred to the end. Integration-level verification (clean multi-target build, XCUITest smoke test, manual accessibility/empty-state walkthrough, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failure paths) happens once at M6, after real repositories replace the fakes.

---

CONSENSUS: YES

phase_discussions.output:
Looking at this discussion, the pattern from all nine prior phases repeats here: Codex is still in cooldown, so it's a single Claude pass — but it's a complete, concrete answer to everything this phase requires, and it doesn't contradict anything locked upstream (project_plan's milestone sequence, tech_specs' owning_lane tags, the interfaces contract). It just turns the milestone plan into file-level ownership and an 18-task backlog with acceptance criteria pulled straight from the locked requirements (threshold-gating test, pinned-timezone DST tests, fail-open StoreKit, HealthKit denial-not-distinguished, WidgetCenter reload only from write paths, etc.).

The one genuinely new decision this round: treating `SomnaApp.swift` as a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain stubs the container → primary_ui wires environment injection → polish_resilience swaps fakes for real repos at integration), with the task dependency graph itself serializing access instead of a lock. That's a sensible, minimal answer to the one real cross-lane conflict risk in an otherwise cleanly-partitioned file layout. The dependency graph is acyclic (verified: T-001 has no deps and every other task chains back to it with no cycles), every task has an owner_lane, and T-001 is startable immediately — satisfying both acceptance checks.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Lane-by-lane responsibilities:**
- **data_domain** — SwiftData models, versioned schema wired into `ModelContainer`, both repository protocols with SwiftData-backed and in-memory implementations, `NightRecord`/`NightRecordAssembler`, and the pairing/DST/edge-case test suite (owns the logic, owns the tests for it).
- **services_utilities** — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, the threshold-gating test (the single most load-bearing deliverable in the app), the seed-data generator, StoreKit/`SubscriptionStore`/`PaywallViewModel`, and `HealthKitImportService`.
- **primary_ui** — design tokens/shared components (`GradientBackground`, `EnergyScaleControl`, etc.) and their tests, `TodayViewModel`/`TodayView`, `InsightsViewModel`/three-card Insights UI, History/Recap view models and views, Settings, and root app/tab wiring.
- **polish_resilience** — Live Activity (ActivityKit), WidgetKit extension, the fakes-to-real-repository integration swap, and the final verification pass (clean multi-target build, XCUITest, manual VoiceOver/empty-state walkthrough, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failure paths). Necessarily starts and finishes last.

**Owned files:** File-level ownership is exclusive per lane, mapped 1:1 to tech_specs' folder layout (`Models/`+`Repositories/` → data_domain, `Engine/`+`Subscriptions/`+`HealthKit/` → services_utilities, `ViewModels/`+`Views/` → primary_ui, `SomnaWidget/`+`LiveActivity/` → polish_resilience). No two lanes own the same file outside the one shared integrator file.

**Handoffs and forbidden edit zones:** `Somna/App/SomnaApp.swift` is a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain's initial container stub → primary_ui's environment-injection wiring → polish_resilience's final fakes-to-real-repo swap), each gated by task dependencies so no two lanes touch it concurrently. `Info.plist` is touched twice (scaffold, then services_utilities' HealthKit usage string), same serialization logic. Two explicit "ready for consumption" handoff signals required beyond milestone dates: data_domain announcing in-memory fakes are ready (unblocks primary_ui), and services_utilities announcing the `CorrelationEngine`'s public shape is stable (unblocks the Insights screen specifically, without stalling Today/History/Recap which only need the fakes).

**Branching/merge:** `lane/data_domain`, `lane/services_utilities`, `lane/primary_ui`, `lane/polish_resilience`, cut only after the M0 scaffold merges to `main` and builds clean on all three targets. data_domain and services_utilities merge independently as milestones clear; primary_ui rebases once data_domain's fakes land; polish_resilience branches last and does the persistence-layer swap directly against integrated `main`, done carefully rather than automatically.

**Testing responsibility:** Gating, not follow-up — each lane's unit tests must pass before its own task is "done" (threshold-gating test for services_utilities, pairing/DST suite for data_domain). Integration-level testing (XCUITest, accessibility, simulated failure paths) is polish_resilience's exclusive job, run once at the end.

phase_discussions.discussion_markdown:
# somna — Task Assignments

_Generated by the autonomous multi-agent orchestrator on 2026-07-08 14:38:27._

## Original Prompt

```
PORTFOLIO_CHILD_PROJECT: true
Parent portfolio: multi-app-exp7
Selected app slug: somna

Build this app as a working local-first SwiftUI MVP.

## Selected App Spec

# Somna

Build mode: **MVP build**.
Build priority: **2**.

## App Name

Somna

## Category

Sleep & Morning Energy

## One-Sentence Promise

Log your sleep and how you feel each morning, and Somna shows you exactly what actually helps you feel rested.

## Target User

Anyone who's tired and curious why some mornings feel great and others don't, without wanting to wear a sleep-tracking ring or let an app record audio all night — students, parents, shift workers, broadly anyone.

## Painful Moment Solved

Waking up exhausted despite 'enough' sleep with no idea whether it was the late coffee, the doomscrolling, or the stress — and existing sleep apps just show a graph with no explanation.

## Why It Is Different

Pairs a 10-second bedtime/wake log with a 10-second morning mood+energy check-in, then surfaces plain-English on-device correlations ('you feel 34% more rested after 7+ hours') instead of raw sleep-stage graphs.

## Why Users Would Pay

Free tier covers logging, streaks, and the weekly recap card; Somna+ unlocks the full correlation engine (caffeine/exercise/screen-time tags vs. sleep quality), unlimited history, and a bedtime Live Activity.

## Subscription Model

Freemium, $4.99/mo or $29.99/yr for Somna+.

## Local-First MVP Scope

SwiftData for nightly logs and morning check-ins; correlation engine computed on-device; optional read-only HealthKit import of sleep duration, never a requirement.

## Future Backend Roadmap

Repository boundary allows swapping the local store for CloudKit sync later; correlation engine stays fully on-device regardless of sync status for privacy.

## AR / Local ML / Local LLM Fit

None required; the correlation engine is simple on-device statistics, the honest and battery-safe choice over sensor-heavy sleep-staging.

## Design Direction

Nocturnal calm gradient system — deep indigo-to-violet dusk gradients lightening toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded components, a moon-phase mark, restful rather than clinical.

## App Store Positioning

Not another sleep-stage graph — Somna tells you what actually helped you feel rested, no ring or overnight mic required.

## Invalidation Criteria

If users keep the bedtime log but stop doing the morning check-in after week 2, the correlation engine has no fuel — signals the check-in flow needs to get shorter or gamified.

## v1 Build Scope

Bedtime/wake logging, morning check-in, weekly recap card + widget, on-device rule-based correlation insights, Somna+ paywall with StoreKit.

## Core Workflows

- log bedtime & wake time
- morning mood/energy check-in with optional tags
- view weekly sleep recap card
- browse plain-English correlation insights
- optional read-only HealthKit import

## iOS-Native Features

- Live Activity for tonight's bedtime countdown
- WidgetKit showing last night's score + tonight's target bedtime
- HealthKit read-only (optional)
- ShareLink for recap card

## Key Risks

- Correlation insights need weeks of data before they're meaningful — needs an honest 'still learning' partial state, not false-confidence insights early
- Read-only HealthKit import is a bonus only — must fully function without HealthKit permission granted

## Claude Design Handoff Prompt

Design a calm sleep & morning-energy app called Somna: deep indigo-to-violet gradient backgrounds that lighten toward soft dawn tones in daytime views, soft-glow rounded cards, a moon-phase mark as the icon motif, minimal typography, restful and quiet rather than clinical or data-dense.

## Parent Portfolio Prompt

Build 5 completely separate, production-ready iOS apps at the same time.

This is a multi-app PORTFOLIO request: multiple apps in one program. Each selected app becomes its own separate project — one folder per app, never bundled together.

Pick any 5 categories/concepts — no fixed assignment. Choose the 5 strongest MASS-MARKET ideas.

TOP PRIORITY — VIRAL, MASS-MARKET APPEAL (hard requirement):
- Every app must target a BROAD, mainstream audience. NO niche-community apps (no hobbyist tools, no professional verticals, no special-interest utilities). If your parents, your barista, and a college student wouldn't all instantly get it, it fails.
- Each app must have a built-in viral loop: something people naturally share, screenshot, send to friends, or compete over (shareable results/cards, streaks, challenges, comparisons, "send this to a friend" moments).
- The concept must be explainable in one sentence and demo-able in 10 seconds.
- Think the scale of habit trackers, photo tools, social utilities, everyday life tools, casual self-improvement, money, food, sleep, relationships, entertainment — things with tens of millions of potential users.

SECOND PRIORITY — DESIGN & UI/UX:
- World-class, distinctive visual identity per app: deliberate color system, type scale, spacing, iconography, motion language — not stock SwiftUI defaults.
- "Apple Design Award" caliber polish. Every screen considered: empty, loading, error states, transitions, haptics, spring animations.
- Dark mode, Dynamic Type, accessibility (VoiceOver, 44pt targets, WCAG AA contrast).
- Each of the 5 apps must look clearly DIFFERENT from the other four — five distinct design directions.
- Documented design system per app (tokens, components, states); built UI must match it.

THIRD PRIORITY — COMPLEXITY & DEPTH:
- Genuinely substantial apps — interconnected features, real domain data model, non-trivial logic, offline persistence with sync-later architecture, robust settings, import/export, and at least one hard technical capability done well.
- Depth that rewards daily use — but never at the expense of instant mass-market clarity.

FOURTH PRIORITY — MONETIZATION:
- Clear, sustainable revenue path per app (subscription tiers, IAP, premium) that feels native to the product, never punitive. Free tier hooks users; paid tier is compelling.

General Requirements:
1. Each app goes from 0 to production-ready.
2. Each app must be unique, useful, and commercially viable, better than competitors in a meaningful way.
3. Local-first, architected so cloud support can be added later without rewriting.

UNIQUENESS (hard rule, non-negotiable):
- No app in this batch may be similar to another app in this batch.
- No app may be similar to anything already built in this workspace: Waylay, TrueScale, Provenance, Scope-Guard, Practice-Loop, Proof, ReturnWise, VerveCoach, CueKeeper, brinekeeper, chalkline, quietpilot, cistern, thicket, undersong, undertow, heddle, marginalia, skyglass, and the ~50 apps of prior batches (aarti-path, afterglow, backtimer, chapterly, cleanline, clearpath-access, crockwatch, crosswire, crux-cycle, curio, dayframe, dharma-trail, docket, flinch, heartline, kitbag, night-sky-logbook, pattern-pulse, pillcheck, referral-loop, risk-compass, rollcall, runway, scenario-ledger, shelf, sincerely, tabzero, talkthrough, tessella, throughline, trestle, triptally, umbra, wordsmith-atlas, and others).
- Produce differentiated concepts even if a category overlaps.

Bonus points (optional, only when integral): unique use of LLMs, AR, or ML.

Build rules:
- Run the five app efforts in parallel; keep phases separate per app.
- TIME MATTERS on this batch: reach consensus quickly in discussion phases (end phases early on agreement), but never skip the build, and never mark an app done unless a real, compiling Xcode project with full source exists in app_build. An app without a buildable Xcode project is a FAILURE.
- Save all phase discussions; combine each app's transcript into a .txt at the end.
- Recover cleanly from stalls; report only important milestones.

Output:
- One folder per app in the output directory — never one folder containing all apps.
Output directory:
- /Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory

Now generate at least 10 candidate ideas, judge them primarily on mass-market viral potential, choose the best 5, and build all 5 to production quality with exceptional UI/UX.
```

## Phase Purpose

Produce concrete workstreams, file ownership, forbidden edit zones, branch names, merge strategy, testing responsibilities, communication protocol, and a conflict-prevention plan.

## Transcript


### Round 1


**Codex — Round 1 (skipped: CLI unavailable)**

_Codex skipped — in cooldown (rate_limit)._

**Claude (Backend / Systems Engineer · Pragmatist) — Round 1**

Coming at this phase the way I'd approach staffing any four-person build against a contract that's already fully specified: the interfaces are locked, the milestones are sequenced, so my job here is just to turn "who owns what" into file-level ownership nobody has to guess about, and to make sure the one shared seam (the app's composition root) doesn't become a merge-conflict magnet.

The app lives at `/Users/pchordia/Documents/iOS-App-Factory/somna/app_build/Somna/` — a single `Somna.xcodeproj` with three targets: `Somna` (app), `SomnaWidget` (widget extension, also hosts the Live Activity), `SomnaTests` (Swift Testing unit tests), plus a `SomnaUITests` XCUITest target added at the end. That's consistent with the sibling apps' `app_build/` convention already in this workspace and with everything tech_specs already locked.

Lane assignments, mapped straight off the tech_specs `owning_lane` tags so there's no re-litigating who owns what type:

`data_domain` owns everything under `Somna/Models/` (both `@Model` types, the value-type structs, `NightRecordAssembler`) and `Somna/Repositories/` (both protocols, the SwiftData-backed implementations, the versioned schema/migration plan, and the in-memory fakes that live in the test target). This lane also owns the pairing/edge-case test suite, because that's the logic it's testing — nobody else should be writing DST or 16-hour-window assertions against a data model they don't own.

`services_utilities` owns `Somna/Engine/` (the `CorrelationEngine` actor, both `InsightRule` implementations, `InsightResult`/`InsightCardData`, the seed-data generator), `Somna/Subscriptions/` (StoreKit manager, entitlement store, the `.storekit` config), and `Somna/HealthKit/`. It owns the threshold-gating test — that test is the single most important deliverable in the whole app and it belongs to the lane that can't ship without it passing.

`primary_ui` owns `Somna/ViewModels/`, `Somna/Views/` (Today, Insights, History, Recap, Settings, and the shared `Components/` — `FrostedCard`, `InsightCard`, `EnergyScaleControl`, `TagChip`, buttons, `StreakBadge`, `MoonCrescentProgress`), and the `gradient(for:)` function plus its unit test. It also owns `RecapData` per tech_specs' lane tag even though Recap feels adjacent to data — that's deliberate, it's a display-projection type, not persistence.

`polish_resilience` owns the entire `SomnaWidget` target, the shared `Somna/LiveActivity/` attribute file, the final integration pass that swaps in-memory fakes for real repositories, and the closing verification sweep (XCUITest, manual accessibility pass, empty/error-state walkthrough, the deliberate StoreKit-failure and HealthKit-denial checks). This lane necessarily starts last and finishes last — there's nothing for it to polish until the other three have something real to integrate.

The one file every lane eventually touches is `Somna/App/SomnaApp.swift` — the `@main` struct that builds the `ModelContainer`, constructs the repositories/engine/subscription store, and injects them via `@Environment`. I'm treating this as a forbidden-edit zone except at three specific, sequenced checkpoints that the task graph already serializes so two lanes are never touching it at once: `data_domain` stubs the `ModelContainer` init early, `primary_ui` does the full environment-injection wiring once the view models exist, and `polish_resilience` does the final swap from fakes to real repos at integration time. Because each of those tasks depends on the previous one finishing, the dependency graph itself is the conflict-prevention mechanism for this file — nobody needs a lock, they just can't start their touch on it until their task unblocks. Same logic covers `Info.plist` (touched once at scaffold, once more by `services_utilities` for the HealthKit usage string).

Branching: one lane branch each off `main` — `lane/data_domain`, `lane/services_utilities`, `lane/primary_ui`, `lane/polish_resilience` — cut only after the M0 scaffold task merges to `main` first and is confirmed building clean on all three targets. `data_domain` and `services_utilities` merge back to `main` as they clear their milestones (they don't depend on each other). `primary_ui` rebases onto `main` once `data_domain`'s repositories/fakes land and merges once its screens compile against them. `polish_resilience` branches last, after `primary_ui` and `services_utilities` are both merged, and does the fakes-to-real-repo swap directly against integrated `main` rather than against a stale branch point — that's the one merge I'd want done carefully rather than automatically, since it's swapping the actual persistence layer under everything.

Testing responsibility follows ownership: each lane's unit tests are a gating part of its own task, not a follow-up — the threshold-gating test has to pass before `services_utilities` calls its engine work done, the pairing/DST suite has to pass before `data_domain` calls its repositories done. Integration-level testing (full-app XCUITest, accessibility pass, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failures) is `polish_resilience`'s job exclusively, run once at the end against the fully-integrated app, per what project_plan already locked.

Communication protocol: since file ownership is exclusive per lane except for the three serialized touches on `SomnaApp.swift`, most work needs zero cross-lane coordination day to day. The two moments that do need a real handoff message rather than just a merge are: `data_domain` announcing its in-memory fakes are ready (that's the actual unblock signal for `primary_ui` to start, not just "M1a is done" as a milestone label), and `services_utilities` announcing the `CorrelationEngine`'s public shape is stable (since `primary_ui`'s `InsightsViewModel` depends on it) — both of those should be explicit "ready for consumption" notes, not assumed from milestone completion dates.

The biggest risk I'd flag against my own plan: I've made `primary_ui` depend on both `data_domain` and `services_utilities` finishing their core pieces before it can build the Insights screen specifically (History/Recap/Today don't need the engine, only Insights does) — if `services_utilities` runs behind, `primary_ui` shouldn't be blocked entirely, just on that one screen. I've reflected that by making Today/History/Recap tasks depend only on `data_domain`'s fakes, and only the Insights task pull in the engine dependency, so a slow engine lane doesn't stall the rest of the UI.

**Coordinator (Claude) — decision after round 1**

Looking at this discussion, the pattern from all nine prior phases repeats here: Codex is still in cooldown, so it's a single Claude pass — but it's a complete, concrete answer to everything this phase requires, and it doesn't contradict anything locked upstream (project_plan's milestone sequence, tech_specs' owning_lane tags, the interfaces contract). It just turns the milestone plan into file-level ownership and an 18-task backlog with acceptance criteria pulled straight from the locked requirements (threshold-gating test, pinned-timezone DST tests, fail-open StoreKit, HealthKit denial-not-distinguished, WidgetCenter reload only from write paths, etc.).

The one genuinely new decision this round: treating `SomnaApp.swift` as a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain stubs the container → primary_ui wires environment injection → polish_resilience swaps fakes for real repos at integration), with the task dependency graph itself serializing access instead of a lock. That's a sensible, minimal answer to the one real cross-lane conflict risk in an otherwise cleanly-partitioned file layout. The dependency graph is acyclic (verified: T-001 has no deps and every other task chains back to it with no cycles), every task has an owner_lane, and T-001 is startable immediately — satisfying both acceptance checks.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Lane-by-lane responsibilities:**
- **data_domain** — SwiftData models, versioned schema wired into `ModelContainer`, both repository protocols with SwiftData-backed and in-memory implementations, `NightRecord`/`NightRecordAssembler`, and the pairing/DST/edge-case test suite (owns the logic, owns the tests for it).
- **services_utilities** — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, the threshold-gating test (the single most load-bearing deliverable in the app), the seed-data generator, StoreKit/`SubscriptionStore`/`PaywallViewModel`, and `HealthKitImportService`.
- **primary_ui** — design tokens/shared components (`GradientBackground`, `EnergyScaleControl`, etc.) and their tests, `TodayViewModel`/`TodayView`, `InsightsViewModel`/three-card Insights UI, History/Recap view models and views, Settings, and root app/tab wiring.
- **polish_resilience** — Live Activity (ActivityKit), WidgetKit extension, the fakes-to-real-repository integration swap, and the final verification pass (clean multi-target build, XCUITest, manual VoiceOver/empty-state walkthrough, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failure paths). Necessarily starts and finishes last.

**Owned files:** File-level ownership is exclusive per lane, mapped 1:1 to tech_specs' folder layout (`Models/`+`Repositories/` → data_domain, `Engine/`+`Subscriptions/`+`HealthKit/` → services_utilities, `ViewModels/`+`Views/` → primary_ui, `SomnaWidget/`+`LiveActivity/` → polish_resilience). No two lanes own the same file outside the one shared integrator file.

**Handoffs and forbidden edit zones:** `Somna/App/SomnaApp.swift` is a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain's initial container stub → primary_ui's environment-injection wiring → polish_resilience's final fakes-to-real-repo swap), each gated by task dependencies so no two lanes touch it concurrently. `Info.plist` is touched twice (scaffold, then services_utilities' HealthKit usage string), same serialization logic. Two explicit "ready for consumption" handoff signals required beyond milestone dates: data_domain announcing in-memory fakes are ready (unblocks primary_ui), and services_utilities announcing the `CorrelationEngine`'s public shape is stable (unblocks the Insights screen specifically, without stalling Today/History/Recap which only need the fakes).

**Branching/merge:** `lane/data_domain`, `lane/services_utilities`, `lane/primary_ui`, `lane/polish_resilience`, cut only after the M0 scaffold merges to `main` and builds clean on all three targets. data_domain and services_utilities merge independently as milestones clear; primary_ui rebases once data_domain's fakes land; polish_resilience branches last and does the persistence-layer swap directly against integrated `main`, done carefully rather than automatically.

**Testing responsibility:** Gating, not follow-up — each lane's unit tests must pass before its own task is "done" (threshold-gating test for services_utilities, pairing/DST suite for data_domain). Integration-level testing (XCUITest, accessibility, simulated failure paths) is polish_resilience's exclusive job, run once at the end.

**Quality Gate (Claude) — after round 1**

QUALITY: PASS

## Feedback
This phase output fully satisfies the required outputs and acceptance checks:

- **Lane-by-lane responsibilities**: All four fixed lanes (data_domain, services_utilities, primary_ui, polish_resilience) have clear, non-overlapping charters that map directly onto the tech_specs `owning_lane` tags and project_plan milestones (M0–M6).
- **Owned files**: File ownership is exclusive per lane, mapped 1:1 to the tech_specs folder layout, with one deliberate, well-justified exception (`SomnaApp.swift` as a serialized shared touchpoint).
- **Handoffs and forbidden edit zones**: `SomnaApp.swift` and `Info.plist` are named forbidden-edit zones outside three sequenced checkpoints, with the dependency graph itself acting as the serialization mechanism — a concrete, non-hand-wavy answer to the conflict-prevention requirement this phase exists to produce. Two explicit "ready for consumption" handoff signals (in-memory fakes ready; CorrelationEngine public shape stable) are named beyond generic milestone dates, and the plan correctly notes that a slow services_utilities lane should only block the Insights task (T-011), not Today/History/Recap (T-010/T-012), which is reflected accurately in the dependency lists.
- **tasks-json backlog**: 18 tasks, each with `id`, `title`, `owner_lane` (valid value in all cases), `files`, `depends_on`, `acceptance_criteria`, and `status`. Verified acyclic: T-001 has no dependencies and every other task chains back through T-001 with no cycle. T-001 is immediately startable, satisfying "build workers can start immediately." No task is ownerless. Acceptance criteria are concrete and carry forward specific non-negotiables from upstream phases (threshold-gating test both branches, pinned-timezone DST tests, fail-open StoreKit, HealthKit denied/not-determined non-distinction, WidgetCenter reload only from write paths, NoSignalCard structural distinctness, 44pt targets, VoiceOver on EnergyScaleControl).

Nothing here contradicts any of the ten prior locked phases — it's a direct, executable translation of project_plan's milestone sequence and tech_specs' interface contract into file-level ownership and a dependency-ordered backlog. Ready to close and move to build.

## Coordinator Decision

See the coordinator's message above.

## Final Output

Looking at this discussion, the pattern from all nine prior phases repeats here: Codex is still in cooldown, so it's a single Claude pass — but it's a complete, concrete answer to everything this phase requires, and it doesn't contradict anything locked upstream (project_plan's milestone sequence, tech_specs' owning_lane tags, the interfaces contract). It just turns the milestone plan into file-level ownership and an 18-task backlog with acceptance criteria pulled straight from the locked requirements (threshold-gating test, pinned-timezone DST tests, fail-open StoreKit, HealthKit denial-not-distinguished, WidgetCenter reload only from write paths, etc.).

The one genuinely new decision this round: treating `SomnaApp.swift` as a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain stubs the container → primary_ui wires environment injection → polish_resilience swaps fakes for real repos at integration), with the task dependency graph itself serializing access instead of a lock. That's a sensible, minimal answer to the one real cross-lane conflict risk in an otherwise cleanly-partitioned file layout. The dependency graph is acyclic (verified: T-001 has no deps and every other task chains back to it with no cycles), every task has an owner_lane, and T-001 is startable immediately — satisfying both acceptance checks.

CONSENSUS: YES

## Final Output

**Lane-by-lane responsibilities:**
- **data_domain** — SwiftData models, versioned schema wired into `ModelContainer`, both repository protocols with SwiftData-backed and in-memory implementations, `NightRecord`/`NightRecordAssembler`, and the pairing/DST/edge-case test suite (owns the logic, owns the tests for it).
- **services_utilities** — `CorrelationEngine` actor, `InsightRule` implementations, the threshold-gating test (the single most load-bearing deliverable in the app), the seed-data generator, StoreKit/`SubscriptionStore`/`PaywallViewModel`, and `HealthKitImportService`.
- **primary_ui** — design tokens/shared components (`GradientBackground`, `EnergyScaleControl`, etc.) and their tests, `TodayViewModel`/`TodayView`, `InsightsViewModel`/three-card Insights UI, History/Recap view models and views, Settings, and root app/tab wiring.
- **polish_resilience** — Live Activity (ActivityKit), WidgetKit extension, the fakes-to-real-repository integration swap, and the final verification pass (clean multi-target build, XCUITest, manual VoiceOver/empty-state walkthrough, simulated StoreKit/HealthKit failure paths). Necessarily starts and finishes last.

**Owned files:** File-level ownership is exclusive per lane, mapped 1:1 to tech_specs' folder layout (`Models/`+`Repositories/` → data_domain, `Engine/`+`Subscriptions/`+`HealthKit/` → services_utilities, `ViewModels/`+`Views/` → primary_ui, `SomnaWidget/`+`LiveActivity/` → polish_resilience). No two lanes own the same file outside the one shared integrator file.

**Handoffs and forbidden edit zones:** `Somna/App/SomnaApp.swift` is a forbidden-edit zone except at three sequenced checkpoints (data_domain's initial container stub → primary_ui's environment-injection wiring → polish_resilience's final fakes-to-real-repo swap), each gated by task dependencies so no two lanes touch it concurrently. `Info.plist` is touched twice (scaffold, then services_utilities' HealthKit usage string), same serialization logic. Two explicit "ready for consumption" handoff signals required beyond milestone dates: data_domain announcing in-memory fakes are ready (unblocks primary_ui), and services_utilities announcing the `CorrelationEngine`'s public shape is stable (unblocks the Insights screen specifically, without stalling Today/History/Recap which only need the fakes).

**Branching/merge:** `lane/data_domain`, `lane/services_utilities`, `lane/primary_ui`, `lane/polish_resilience`, cut only after the M0 scaffold merges to `main` and builds clean on all three targets. data_domain and services_utilities merge independently as milestones clear; primary_ui rebases once data_domain's fakes land; polish_resilience branches last and does the persistence-layer swap directly against integrated `main`, done carefully rather than automatically.

**Testing responsibility:** Gating, not follow-up — each lane's unit tests must pass before its own task is "done" (threshold-gating test for services_utilities, pairing/DST suite for data_domain). Integration-level testing (XCUITest, accessibility, simulated failure paths) is polish_resilience's exclusive job, run once at the end.
